syllabus_desc: Describe key features of good and safe airmanship.
concept: Good airmanship isn't about having the shiniest aircraft or the fanciest flying skills - it's about consistently making sound decisions that keep you and your passengers safe. Think of it as the bedrock of everything you do in the cockpit. Safe airmanship means knowing your limits, respecting weather, maintaining your aircraft to spec, and never, ever cutting corners. When you're flying from Ardmore up to Hamilton, you're constantly making micro-decisions: checking fuel consumption, watching cloud development, assessing your escape options. That's airmanship in action. It's also about your attitude - staying humble, admitting when you don't know something, and being willing to ask for help on the radio. The best pilots are the ones who treat every flight like it matters, who brief properly, who stick to procedures even when they seem tedious, and who go home safe every time.
exam_tip: Examiners want to hear that good airmanship is a mindset - proactive, humble, and safety-focused. Mention specific habits like thorough briefing, weather assessment, and never pressuring yourself into bad conditions.
key_facts: Airmanship encompasses judgment, decision-making, and adherence to procedures
Knowing your personal limits prevents accidents caused by overconfidence
Proper briefing and pre-flight planning are non-negotiable aspects of safe flying
Attitude and humility are as important as technical skill
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA General Aviation Publication

syllabus_desc: Define human factors as used in an aviation context.
concept: Human factors in aviation is basically the study of how pilots, engineers, mechanics, and controllers interact with the machines, procedures, and environment around them - and where things go wrong. It's not about blame; it's about understanding why humans make mistakes and designing systems that catch those mistakes before they become accidents. In the cockpit, you're constantly managing workload, fatigue, stress, and distraction. You might be brilliant at instrument flying, but if you're exhausted after a long duty day, your decision-making suffers. Human factors recognizes that error is inevitable - the trick is building redundancy and checklists so one mistake doesn't cascade into disaster. When you're flying into Milford Sound on a marginal day, managing wind shear, narrow valleys, and high workload, human factors training teaches you to prioritize, delegate to your crew, and know when to wave off and come back another day.
exam_tip: Define human factors as the science of human performance in aviation, not just 'knowing yourself.' Mention the integrated nature - people, equipment, and environment all interact.
key_facts: Human factors studies the interaction between humans, machines, and the environment in aviation
It acknowledges that errors are inevitable and systems should be designed to mitigate them
Workload, fatigue, stress, and distraction directly affect pilot decision-making and performance
Error management and procedural discipline prevent accidents
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Human Factors in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Describe the fundamentals of the SHEL Model in relation to the interaction of humans with other humans, hardware, information sources and the environment.
concept: The SHEL Model is a handy framework that organizes human factors into four layers. S-H-E-L: Software (procedures, rules, documentation), Hardware (aircraft systems, instruments, controls), Environment (weather, terrain, airspace), and Liveware (the humans - you and your crew). The magic is in how they interact. You might have perfect procedures (Software) and a reliable aircraft (Hardware), but if you're flying into deteriorating weather near the Kaingaroa Forest (Environment) and you're exhausted (Liveware), the system breaks down. Good training and operational design minimize mismatches. For instance, if your altitude selector on the autopilot is small and hard to read, that's Hardware-Liveware mismatch - your eyes and attention can't interface well with it. If ATC procedures don't account for the natural communication delays at certain aerodromes, that's Software-Liveware mismatch. Understanding SHEL helps you spot weak points in any operation and fix them before they cause incidents.
exam_tip: Use a concrete example - your aircraft, your workload, the weather, and the procedures. Show how mismatch between any two layers (like difficult-to-read instruments during high workload) creates risk.
key_facts: SHEL: Software (procedures), Hardware (aircraft systems), Environment (weather/terrain), Liveware (humans)
Poor interfaces between any two layers create accident precursors
System redesign focuses on improving these interfaces rather than blaming individuals
Effective operations ensure all four layers complement each other
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, SHEL Model in Aviation

syllabus_desc: State the gases that make up the atmosphere.
concept: The atmosphere is mostly nitrogen and oxygen, with smaller amounts of argon, carbon dioxide, and trace gases. Nitrogen makes up about 78%, oxygen about 21%, and everything else (argon, CO2, neon, helium, methane) fills the last 1%. You need to know this because it directly affects how your body processes oxygen at altitude, how your engine breathes, and how weather develops. Oxygen is the critical one for you as a pilot - it's what your blood needs, and it's what your engines need for combustion. When you climb above a few thousand feet, oxygen availability becomes a real factor, especially if you're flying unpressurized. At Wanaka or Queenstown, even though you're not technically high by jet standards, the reduced oxygen partial pressure starts affecting your performance. Understanding the basic composition helps you appreciate why oxygen systems exist, why hypoxia is a real threat, and why meteorology works the way it does.
exam_tip: Memorize the key percentages: 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, 1% other. Know why oxygen is critical for your physiology and engine operation.
key_facts: Nitrogen: ~78%, Oxygen: ~21%, Argon and other trace gases: ~1%
Oxygen availability decreases with altitude, affecting both human and engine performance
The ratio remains constant up to about 80 km, though density decreases
Carbon dioxide, though only ~0.04%, plays a role in weather formation and human physiology

syllabus_desc: State the percentage of each gas in the atmosphere.
concept: The atmosphere's composition is remarkably stable, at least in the layers where we fly. Nitrogen (N₂) dominates at 78%, providing inert structure. Oxygen (O₂) comprises 21%, and it's the only one you really care about physiologically. The remaining 1% includes argon (0.93%), carbon dioxide (0.04%), and trace amounts of neon, helium, methane, krypton, and hydrogen. These percentages matter because they don't change much - whether you're flying out of Auckland, Wellington, or over the Canterbury Plains, the air composition is the same. What does change is the partial pressure of oxygen, which depends on total atmospheric pressure and altitude. That's why this number - 21% oxygen - is so important: multiply it by the total atmospheric pressure at your altitude, and you get the partial pressure of oxygen your body actually receives. At sea level, that's helpful. At 10,000 feet, it becomes downright concerning without supplemental oxygen. The stability of these percentages means the atmosphere is predictable; your engines and your body know what to expect.
exam_tip: Recite the percentages accurately. Then explain that it's not the percentage that changes with altitude - it's the total pressure that drops, making each gas's partial pressure drop too.
key_facts: Nitrogen 78%, Oxygen 21%, Argon 0.93%, CO₂ 0.04%, trace gases <0.01%
These percentages remain constant up to approximately 80 km altitude
Partial pressure of oxygen = oxygen percentage × total atmospheric pressure
Oxygen percentage is constant; partial pressure decreases with altitude

syllabus_desc: Describe the anatomy and physiology of the respiratory system.
concept: Your respiratory system is your direct pipeline to oxygen. Air enters through your nose or mouth, travels down the windpipe (trachea), splits into two main bronchi (one to each lung), and branches into smaller bronchioles, finally ending in millions of tiny air sacs called alveoli. That's where the magic happens - oxygen crosses from the alveoli into your blood, and carbon dioxide crosses back out to be exhaled. The lungs are elastic tissue surrounded by the pleural membrane, and they're anchored to your rib cage. Your diaphragm - a large muscle underneath your lungs - does most of the work of breathing when you're at rest. When you're stressed or exercising (like during a challenging flight into challenging terrain), your intercostal muscles (between your ribs) pitch in to boost ventilation. Your breathing rate and depth are controlled by your brain, which monitors blood carbon dioxide and oxygen levels through chemoreceptors. It's an elegant system, but it has limits - which is why altitude, hyperventilation, and stress can all throw it off.
exam_tip: Draw the pathway and label the key structures. Explain how oxygen gets into the blood and CO₂ gets out. This is foundational for understanding hypoxia and hyperventilation.
key_facts: Air pathway: nose → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli
Alveoli are where gas exchange occurs between air and blood
The diaphragm is the primary breathing muscle; intercostal muscles assist during exertion
Breathing is controlled by the brain, responding to CO₂ and O₂ levels
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Describe the anatomy and physiology of the circulatory system.
concept: Your heart pumps oxygen-rich blood through your arteries to every cell in your body, and oxygen-poor blood returns through your veins to be refreshed by the lungs. The heart itself is a muscular pump with four chambers: the right side receives deoxygenated blood from the body and pumps it to the lungs; the left side receives oxygenated blood from the lungs and pumps it to the body. Valves keep blood flowing one direction. Your arteries are thick-walled and muscular, branching into smaller arterioles and eventually capillaries, where oxygen actually transfers to cells. Capillaries rejoin into venules and veins, which return blood to the heart. The whole circuit depends on pressure - your heart maintains it through its pumping action. Blood volume and viscosity (thickness) also matter; dehydration or high altitude can thicken your blood, making your heart work harder. Stress, exertion, and anxiety all increase your heart rate and blood pressure, which is why staying calm in the cockpit is physiologically important, not just a nice idea. A well-oxygenated circulatory system is non-negotiable for sharp thinking and steady hands when you're approaching Milford Sound in marginal conditions.
exam_tip: Explain the double circulation: pulmonary (heart to lungs) and systemic (heart to body). Emphasize that oxygen delivery depends on both heart function and adequate oxygen in the blood.
key_facts: Heart has four chambers: right atrium/ventricle pump to lungs, left atrium/ventricle pump to body
Arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart; veins return oxygen-poor blood
Gas exchange occurs in capillaries at the tissue level
Heart rate and blood pressure increase with stress, exertion, and hypoxia
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Describe the role of the lungs in oxygen and carbon dioxide transfer.
concept: Your lungs are essentially a gas exchange factory. Every time you breathe, fresh air reaches the alveoli - those millions of tiny air sacs with incredibly thin walls. Oxygen from that air diffuses across the alveolar membrane into the blood in the surrounding capillaries. At the same time, carbon dioxide (a waste product your cells have produced) diffuses from the blood into the alveoli and gets exhaled. This two-way exchange is passive diffusion - it happens because of concentration gradients, not because anything is 'pumping' the gases. The larger the surface area available for exchange, the more efficient the transfer. Your lungs have an enormous surface area (about 70 square meters if you spread out all the alveoli), which is why normal breathing at sea level gets your blood well-oxygenated. But at altitude, even though your lungs are working fine anatomically, the partial pressure of oxygen is lower, so oxygen diffuses more slowly into the blood. That's the beginning of hypoxia. Your lungs also have chemoreceptors that sense CO₂ levels and trigger you to breathe faster if CO₂ builds up - which is why hyperventilation is a real phenomenon when you're anxious.
exam_tip: Explain that diffusion is passive and depends on partial pressure gradients. This is why altitude causes hypoxia even though oxygen percentage stays at 21%.
key_facts: Gas exchange in the lungs is passive diffusion across the alveolar membrane
Oxygen moves from alveoli into blood; CO₂ moves from blood into alveoli
Lungs have ~70 m² of surface area for gas exchange
Partial pressure of oxygen, not percentage, determines diffusion rate into blood
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Explain the role of human factors programmes in promoting aviation safety.
concept: Human factors programmes are structured efforts to recognize, analyze, and eliminate the human-system mismatches that cause accidents. They exist at every level: regulatory (CAA requirements), organizational (your flight school's procedures), and individual (your own error-management habits). A good programme combines training, reporting systems, and design improvements. For example, if pilots keep accidentally selecting the wrong altimeter setting on the autopilot, the programme might include recurrent training, a clearer checklist, and hardware redesign to make the control more intuitive. In New Zealand, DAME (Deferential Assessment of the Medical Examination) is one human factors tool - it recognizes that rigid medical rules can drive pilots to hide problems rather than seek help. Modern human factors programmes encourage a 'just culture,' where people report errors without fear of punishment, so the organization can learn and improve. Flight training schools like Stoneybrook use briefing, debriefing, and scenario-based training to develop your error-detection and recovery skills. The result is a safer system because errors are caught before they cascade. Every accident investigation done by the TAIC (Transport Accident Investigation Commission) emphasizes human factors learnings that feed back into these programmes.
exam_tip: Give a concrete example of how a human factors programme prevents accidents - maybe poor checklist design or fatigue policies. Show that it's not about blame; it's about system improvement.
key_facts: Human factors programmes aim to identify and eliminate human-system mismatches
They operate at regulatory, organizational, and individual levels
Just culture encourages reporting without fear, enabling organizational learning
Training, design, and procedures are all levers for safety improvement
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Human Factors in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Describe the variation of pressure as altitude increases.
concept: Atmospheric pressure decreases exponentially as you climb - it's not a linear drop. At sea level, pressure is about 101.3 kPa (14.7 psi, or 760 mmHg). Every time you climb roughly 1,000 feet, pressure drops about 3.6%. By 5,500 feet, it's down to 75% of sea level. At 18,000 feet (the flight level where unpressurized aircraft get into real trouble), it's about 50% of sea level. The reason is gravity: the atmosphere gets thinner as you move away from Earth's pull, and there's less 'weight' of air above you pressing down. This is why your altimeter works - it measures pressure and converts that to altitude. The exponential drop is important because it affects both your physiology and your aircraft's performance. Your engine produces less power because there's less oxygen available. Your body struggles to extract oxygen from air that has lower partial pressure. The rate of pressure change also matters: a rapid altitude chamber decompression is far more dangerous than a gradual climb, even to the same altitude. Understanding this curve helps you grasp why 10,000 feet unpressurized is risky but manageable, while 20,000 feet without pressurization or oxygen is genuinely dangerous very quickly.
exam_tip: Sketch the exponential curve. Explain that the drop accelerates initially and then levels off, and that this affects both your body's oxygen extraction and your aircraft's performance.
key_facts: Pressure decreases exponentially with altitude, not linearly
Sea level: ~101.3 kPa; halves around 18,000 feet
Roughly 3.6% pressure drop per 1,000 feet
Lower pressure means lower partial pressure of oxygen, affecting both physiology and engine performance

syllabus_desc: Define hypoxia.
concept: Hypoxia is an oxygen deficiency in your body's tissues. It doesn't mean there's no oxygen - it means there's not enough to support normal function. Your brain and heart are most sensitive; they're constantly demanding oxygen and they degrade quickly without it. Hypoxia can develop in several ways: not enough oxygen in the air you're breathing (which is the main risk at altitude), your lungs not extracting oxygen properly, your blood not carrying enough oxygen, or your tissues not being able to use the oxygen they receive. For pilots, altitude hypoxia is the primary concern. When you climb unpressurized above about 10,000 feet, you're gradually becoming hypoxic, though you might not realize it because hypoxia doesn't always announce itself with obvious symptoms. The insidious thing about hypoxia is that it impairs judgment and self-awareness - exactly the tools you need to recognize you're hypoxic. You might feel fine while your cognitive performance is already degraded. That's why oxygen systems, proper climb profiles, and regular medical checks (CAANZ medical certificates) are non-negotiable. In NZ, flying unpressurized above 10,000 feet without supplemental oxygen is a CAR Part 91 violation - the rules exist because hypoxia kills quietly.
exam_tip: Define it clearly: tissue oxygen deficiency, not absence of oxygen. Emphasize that hypoxia is insidious because it degrades your judgment before you notice symptoms.
key_facts: Hypoxia: inadequate oxygen delivery to tissues
Brain and heart are most vulnerable; they show symptoms first
Altitude hypoxia occurs when partial pressure of oxygen is too low
Hypoxia impairs judgment, making self-recognition difficult

syllabus_desc: State the partial pressure of oxygen both inside and outside the lungs at sea level.
concept: At sea level, total atmospheric pressure is about 101.3 kPa. The air you breathe is 21% oxygen, so the partial pressure of oxygen in the atmosphere is 0.21 × 101.3 = about 21.3 kPa (or 160 mmHg). However, the air that reaches your alveoli is not the same as ambient air. By the time air travels down your trachea and bronchi, it's mixed with water vapor from your airways and the residual air already in your lungs. Water vapor has a partial pressure of about 6.3 kPa, so the partial pressure of oxygen in your alveoli at sea level is actually lower: roughly 21.3 - 6.3 = about 15 kPa (or 100-110 mmHg, depending on how much CO₂ is also in your lungs). This means oxygen crosses from your alveoli (15 kPa) into your venous blood (which is around 5 kPa in the pulmonary capillaries), so diffusion happens readily. The key insight is that alveolar oxygen pressure, not atmospheric oxygen pressure, is what matters for your blood oxygenation. At altitude, the atmospheric oxygen partial pressure drops, so alveolar oxygen pressure drops even more, and hypoxia begins. This is why understanding these numbers helps you appreciate why 10,000 feet starts to become risky.
exam_tip: Learn both numbers and understand why they differ. The difference - water vapor in your lungs - is critical. At altitude, both drop, but alveolar pressure drops further, worsening hypoxia.
key_facts: Atmospheric O₂ partial pressure at sea level: ~21.3 kPa (21% of 101.3 kPa)
Alveolar O₂ partial pressure at sea level: ~15 kPa (reduced by water vapor in airways)
Water vapor pressure in lungs: ~6.3 kPa
Diffusion gradient from alveoli (~15 kPa) to venous blood (~5 kPa) drives oxygen uptake

syllabus_desc: Explain the mechanical effect of the partial pressure of oxygen on oxygen transfer in the lungs.
concept: Oxygen moves from your lungs into your blood purely because of the partial pressure gradient - the difference in oxygen concentration between air and blood. At sea level, alveolar oxygen is ~15 kPa and venous blood entering your lungs is ~5 kPa, creating a 10 kPa gradient that drives diffusion. The higher the alveolar partial pressure, the faster oxygen diffuses into your blood. At altitude, the atmospheric oxygen partial pressure drops (because total pressure drops), so alveolar oxygen partial pressure drops too. At 10,000 feet, total pressure is roughly 70 kPa, oxygen partial pressure is about 0.21 × 70 = 14.7 kPa, and alveolar oxygen might be around 8-9 kPa after accounting for water vapor. Now your gradient is only 3-4 kPa instead of 10 kPa - diffusion slows dramatically. Your blood becomes less oxygenated even though you're breathing 21% oxygen. This mechanical reality is why supplemental oxygen becomes critical at altitude: it raises alveolar oxygen back up, restoring a healthy diffusion gradient. It's not magic; it's simple physics. Understanding this helps you see why oxygen systems aren't luxury - they're essential safety equipment, and checking them is as important as checking your fuel.
exam_tip: Explain the mechanics: lower atmospheric pressure → lower alveolar partial pressure → slower diffusion → less oxygen in blood. Supplemental oxygen fixes the last link in this chain.
key_facts: Oxygen diffuses passively down the partial pressure gradient
Diffusion rate is proportional to the gradient between alveoli and blood
At altitude, lower atmospheric pressure means lower alveolar oxygen partial pressure
Supplemental oxygen restores the diffusion gradient at altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of hypoxia.
concept: Hypoxia can develop through several mechanisms. Altitude hypoxia - what we mostly worry about as pilots - happens when you climb high enough that atmospheric oxygen partial pressure drops below what your lungs can extract efficiently. Even though you're breathing 21% oxygen all the way, the total pressure is so low that your alveolar partial pressure of oxygen becomes insufficient. This is the main risk above 10,000 feet unpressurized. Histotoxic hypoxia occurs when your tissues can't use oxygen properly, even if blood oxygen is normal. Alcohol, some drugs, and cyanide poisoning cause this. Anemic hypoxia happens when your blood can't carry oxygen - after significant blood loss or with anemia. Circulatory hypoxia (or stagnant hypoxia) occurs when blood isn't circulating properly to tissues, like in shock or extreme cold. For pilots, altitude hypoxia is the real threat, but understanding the others matters because stress, dehydration, and poor physical condition can make you more susceptible. A pilot flying unpressurized from Hamilton toward Taupo at 12,000 feet is experiencing altitude hypoxia. If that same pilot is dehydrated and hungover (reducing their anemia tolerance), they become hypoxic faster. Knowing the causes helps you build safeguards: climb gradually, use oxygen, stay hydrated, avoid alcohol before flying.
exam_tip: Focus on altitude hypoxia as the pilot's main concern, but know all four types. Explain that vulnerability depends on both the cause and the pilot's physical condition.
key_facts: Altitude hypoxia: insufficient oxygen partial pressure in air breathed
Histotoxic hypoxia: tissues cannot use available oxygen (drugs, toxins)
Anemic hypoxia: blood cannot carry enough oxygen (blood loss, anemia)
Circulatory hypoxia: blood not reaching tissues (shock, severe cold)

syllabus_desc: Describe the common symptoms of hypoxia.
concept: Hypoxia symptoms are frustratingly non-specific, and they develop gradually, making them easy to miss. Early signs include euphoria (yes, really - you might feel oddly confident), headache, and mild fatigue. As hypoxia worsens, you might experience tingling in your fingers and lips, dizziness, and visual blurring. Your thinking slows: you struggle with complex tasks, make poor decisions, and your scan becomes sloppy. Some people report a sense of well-being or light-headedness. Your lips and fingernails might have a bluish tint (cyanosis), though this is a late sign and you might not notice it on yourself. Reaction time deteriorates, and your night vision suffers early. The really dangerous part? Hypoxia erodes your self-awareness. You might think you're flying fine while you're actually making sloppy turns and missing instrument calls. In a crisis, you're less able to recognize the problem and take action. Personality changes can occur - someone normally calm might become irritable. At very high altitudes, there's a period of 'apparent well-being' before loss of consciousness, which is particularly dangerous because you trust your judgment right when it's most compromised. This is why supplemental oxygen, regular checks, and never pushing altitude limits are crucial. Symptoms vary by individual and by speed of ascent.
exam_tip: List symptoms in order of progression. Emphasize that early symptoms are mild and insidious. Explain that hypoxia tricks you into thinking you're fine - that's the real danger.
key_facts: Early: euphoria, headache, fatigue, tingling
Progressive: blurred vision, poor coordination, slowed thinking, poor judgment
Late: cyanosis, confusion, unconsciousness
Hypoxia impairs self-awareness, making it hard to recognize the problem

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons hypoxia symptoms are difficult to detect.
concept: Hypoxia is a sneaky adversary because its symptoms directly affect the very faculties you'd use to detect it - your judgment, self-awareness, and attention. Your prefrontal cortex, responsible for rational thought and self-monitoring, is exquisitely sensitive to low oxygen. As hypoxia develops, you lose the ability to objectively assess your own performance. You might feel fine - even euphoric - while your cognitive abilities are already noticeably degraded. Observers on the ground or your crew might spot that something's wrong before you do. Additionally, symptoms are non-specific: is that dizziness altitude hypoxia, or just motion sickness? Are you euphoric because you're hypoxic, or because you're happy about finishing your approach? Hypoxia develops gradually at modest altitudes, so there's no sudden alarm bell - it creeps up. Your body can tolerate moderate hypoxia for a while, so there's a deceptive period where you function reasonably but not optimally. Different people react differently: one pilot might get tingling and headache, another might experience only irritability. This variability means you can't rely on 'I'll know when something's wrong.' The only reliable defense is procedural - know your limits, use oxygen before you need it, climb gradually so your body can acclimatize, and have a crew or instructor who can spot changes in your behavior.
exam_tip: Explain the chicken-and-egg problem: hypoxia degrades the very awareness you need to detect it. This is why procedures and oxygen systems are not optional - they're your only reliable defense.
key_facts: Hypoxia impairs judgment before symptoms become obvious
Early symptoms are mild and can be mistaken for other causes
Different individuals show different symptoms at the same altitude
Gradual onset makes detection harder than sudden decompression

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between hypoxic onset and both vision and cognitive performance.
concept: Vision is one of the first systems to degrade when hypoxia sets in, and cognitive performance follows closely behind. Night vision is particularly vulnerable - at altitudes above 5,000 feet, your night vision starts suffering even though you might feel fine. Your color vision also deteriorates, and your peripheral vision narrows. These changes happen because your retina is metabolically demanding and has high oxygen requirements. By the time you notice blurred vision or color desaturation, your cognitive performance is already compromised. Your ability to do complex tasks - managing the autopilot, deciphering a complex chart, problem-solving in an emergency - degrades before obvious cognitive symptoms appear. Reaction time slows, attention wavers, and decision-making becomes risky. You might make subtle errors in instrument interpretation or miss radio calls. The timeline matters: at 10,000 feet unpressurized, vision effects might take 30 minutes to become noticeable; cognitive degradation starts earlier but is harder to perceive. At 15,000 feet, the timeline accelerates dramatically. In a pressurized aircraft, this isn't an issue because cabin pressure is maintained. In unpressurized aircraft, it's a key reason why staying below 10,000 feet without supplemental oxygen is the prudent rule. Understanding that vision and cognition degrade together helps you appreciate why checklists, briefings, and procedures are so important - they substitute for the judgment you're losing.
exam_tip: Explain that vision loss is an early, measurable sign of hypoxia onset. Cognitive degradation is simultaneous but harder to detect. This is why you don't wait until you feel obviously cognitive symptoms - you respect altitude limits.
key_facts: Night vision begins degrading above 5,000 feet
Color vision and peripheral vision narrow with hypoxia
Cognitive degradation (reaction time, decision-making) occurs alongside vision loss
Hypoxic effects accelerate exponentially above 10,000 feet unpressurized

syllabus_desc: Describe how hypoxia can be prevented.
concept: Preventing hypoxia is straightforward in principle: keep your blood oxygen at healthy levels. The main strategies are staying low, using supplemental oxygen, or flying a pressurized aircraft. For most pilots, staying low is the practical default. CAR Part 91 requires that unpressurized aircraft must use supplemental oxygen above 10,000 feet, and logically, you should be using oxygen even before that - start at 8,000 feet to be safe, or at least have it ready. Regular aerobic fitness helps your cardiovascular system deliver oxygen more efficiently. Staying hydrated improves blood viscosity and oxygen-carrying capacity. Avoiding alcohol, drugs, and fatigue beforehand reduces your vulnerability; a tired, hungover pilot is more susceptible to hypoxic effects than a rested, healthy one. Gradual ascent (rather than a rapid climb) gives your body time to partially acclimate. For flights to high terrain like the mountains around Wanaka or the Southern Alps, planning your oxygen strategy before departure is essential. Knowing your aircraft's service ceiling and your own physiological limits matters. Regular medical checks through CAANZ ensure you don't have underlying conditions (anemia, cardiac issues, etc.) that make hypoxia more dangerous. If you're prone to motion sickness or anxiety, managing these before flight reduces respiratory stress. The principle is layered defense: respect altitude limits, use oxygen, stay fit, plan carefully, and never push your limits to find out where they actually are.
exam_tip: List practical prevention strategies: altitude limits, supplemental oxygen, fitness, sleep, hydration. Emphasize that preventing hypoxia is a series of small decisions, not one big action.
key_facts: Stay below 10,000 feet unpressurized, use oxygen above 8,000 feet
Gradual ascent allows partial acclimatization
Physical fitness, hydration, and sleep improve oxygen tolerance
Know your aircraft's service ceiling and your physiological limits
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91, Altitude-Related Hypoxia Prevention

syllabus_desc: State the factors that affect the likelihood of suffering from hypoxia.
concept: Your individual risk of hypoxia at a given altitude depends on multiple factors. Age matters: older pilots might be slightly more vulnerable, particularly if they have underlying cardiovascular or respiratory issues. Physical fitness is huge - a pilot in good aerobic condition tolerates altitude better than a sedentary one. Pre-existing medical conditions (anemia, heart disease, respiratory disease) reduce your hypoxia tolerance significantly. Any condition that affects oxygen carrying (anemia, blood disorders) or oxygen utilization (some neurological conditions) increases risk. Medications can affect tolerance too - antihistamines cause drowsiness, which exacerbates hypoxic symptoms. Stress and anxiety elevate your metabolic rate and breathing rate, which can contribute to hyperventilation or worsening hypoxia. Fatigue is a major factor: a tired pilot's brain demands oxygen more desperately and is more vulnerable to hypoxic effects. Dehydration thickens blood and reduces oxygen delivery. Smoking damages your lungs' efficiency and reduces your oxygen saturation baseline. Alcohol consumption the night before flying reduces your hypoxia tolerance - your blood's oxygen-carrying capacity is compromised. Pregnancy increases metabolic demands and can reduce tolerance. The rate of ascent matters: a rapid climb exposes you to hypoxia faster than a gradual ascent, giving your body less time to partially adjust. Your individual genetics play a role too - some people are naturally more resistant to hypoxic effects than others. The upshot? Respect altitude limits regardless of how fit you feel. Don't push into marginal territory hoping your fitness will compensate.
exam_tip: Identify factors under your control (fitness, sleep, hydration, avoiding alcohol) and factors that aren't (age, genetics, pre-existing conditions). Emphasize that even factors you can't control shouldn't be ignored - they inform your decision-making.
key_facts: Physical fitness, sleep, and hydration improve hypoxia tolerance
Anemia, heart disease, and respiratory disease reduce tolerance
Stress, fatigue, and anxiety worsen hypoxic effects
Rapid ascent exposes you to hypoxia faster than gradual climb

syllabus_desc: Describe how hypoxia can be treated.
concept: If you find yourself hypoxic - though you shouldn't because you're respecting altitude limits and using oxygen - the treatment is immediate and simple: increase your oxygen partial pressure. That means descending, deploying supplemental oxygen, or both. Immediate descent is the most direct fix. If you're in an unpressurized aircraft at 12,000 feet and suddenly aware of symptoms, start descending immediately. Losing 5,000 feet in altitude can dramatically restore your oxygen levels. If you have supplemental oxygen onboard, deploy it immediately - even if you've already started descending, oxygen recovery is faster if you're breathing rich oxygen while climbing back to safer altitudes. The specific treatment depends on the type of hypoxia. Altitude hypoxia (the main pilot concern) is fixed by descending or using oxygen. Anemic hypoxia (not enough hemoglobin to carry oxygen) doesn't improve with supplemental oxygen, though oxygen helps a bit. Circulatory hypoxia requires treating the underlying circulation problem - medical help. Histotoxic hypoxia (tissues can't use oxygen, like alcohol poisoning) isn't fixed by supplemental oxygen; the toxin must be cleared from your system. In practice, for pilots, if you suspect hypoxia, treat it as altitude hypoxia and descend or use oxygen. Recovery is usually rapid - within minutes of increasing oxygen availability, your cognition clears, your vision normalizes, and you feel better. The real lesson? Don't get there in the first place. Respect altitude limits, use oxygen proactively, and never rely on recognizing hypoxia's insidious onset.
exam_tip: Explain the immediate action: descend and/or use oxygen. Emphasize that treatment is simple only because you should never let hypoxia develop - prevention through procedures is the real defense.
key_facts: Immediate treatment for altitude hypoxia: descend or use supplemental oxygen
Recovery is usually rapid once oxygen partial pressure increases
Different hypoxia types require different treatments
Prevention through altitude limits and oxygen is infinitely better than treatment

syllabus_desc: Define hyperventilation.
concept: Hyperventilation is rapid, deep breathing that exceeds your body's metabolic needs. Normally, you breathe at a rate that balances oxygen intake with carbon dioxide exhaust - your brain's chemoreceptors monitor CO₂ levels and adjust your breathing to keep them stable. When you hyperventilate, you're expelling CO₂ faster than your body is producing it, so CO₂ levels in your blood drop. This sounds like it should be good (more oxygen, less CO₂), but it's actually problematic because low CO₂ affects your blood pH and your nervous system function. Hyperventilation is common in pilots because the cockpit environment is stressful. You're anxious about weather, ATC, or traffic, your sympathetic nervous system activates (fight-or-flight response), and you start breathing faster almost without noticing. Before long, you're hyperventilating. Unlike hypoxia, which impairs judgment, hyperventilation typically makes you acutely aware something's wrong - you feel dizzy, your fingers and lips tingle, you might have chest tightness or palpitations. It's not subtle. The danger of hyperventilation isn't that it kills you; it's that it impairs your ability to fly the aircraft and make decisions during the crisis that triggered the hyperventilation in the first place. A pilot panicking in marginal weather who hyperventilates becomes even less capable of handling the situation. The good news? It's entirely preventable and quickly reversible with conscious breathing control.
exam_tip: Contrast hyperventilation with hypoxia: hyperventilation is obvious and stress-induced; hypoxia is insidious and altitude-induced. Both degrade performance, but in different ways.
key_facts: Hyperventilation: breathing rate/depth exceeds metabolic needs
Results in excessive CO₂ loss, lowering blood CO₂ and pH
Usually triggered by stress, anxiety, or panic
Causes dizziness, tingling, palpitations - very noticeable (unlike hypoxia)

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of hyperventilation.
concept: Hyperventilation in the cockpit is almost always stress-induced. Fear of weather, ATC communication anxiety, turbulence, an engine problem, or simply the mental load of an unfamiliar approach can trigger your sympathetic nervous system - your fight-or-flight response. Your heart rate climbs, your muscles tense, and your breathing accelerates almost involuntarily. You're not consciously deciding to hyperventilate; it's a physiological response to perceived threat. The more anxious you are, the worse it gets. A pilot flying into marginal weather at Milford Sound who's already nervous about wind shear and turbulence might unconsciously start breathing faster. In the cockpit, the combination of workload, noise, and decision pressure amplifies this response. Some people are naturally more prone to anxiety-driven hyperventilation, especially if they struggle with anxiety generally or if they're tired, hungry, or dehydrated beforehand. Medications that affect anxiety can increase vulnerability. Inexperience is a big factor: a new pilot tackling a challenging approach is more likely to hyperventilate than a seasoned one. Pain or illness can also trigger hyperventilation - if you have a headache or feel unwell before the flight, you're more vulnerable. Unlike hypoxia, which is purely physical (altitude exposure), hyperventilation is genuinely psychological. Your mind perceives threat, your nervous system responds, and breathing rate increases. Understanding this is key: managing hyperventilation means managing the anxiety, not just treating the symptom.
exam_tip: Emphasize the stress connection. Give an example: a new pilot on an IFR approach in moderate turbulence is more likely to hyperventilate than the same pilot on a calm day. The physiology is triggered by psychology.
key_facts: Hyperventilation is triggered by stress, anxiety, or perceived threat
Sympathetic nervous system activation increases breathing rate involuntarily
Inexperience, fatigue, and pre-existing anxiety increase vulnerability
Psychological state (not just altitude) drives hyperventilation

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of hyperventilation.
concept: Hyperventilation symptoms are very noticeable because they develop quickly and feel uncomfortable. The hallmark sign is tingling in your fingertips, lips, and around your mouth - this is caused by the pH change in your blood from lost CO₂. You typically feel dizzy or lightheaded. Your fingers might go numb or feel cold. Your chest might feel tight, and you might experience palpitations (awareness of your heartbeat, which is usually faster because you're stressed). Some people report a sensation of choking or inability to catch their breath, which paradoxically makes them breathe even faster. You might feel apprehensive or panicky (which reinforces the cycle). Your thoughts might become scattered, and concentration becomes difficult. Some people experience muscle cramps or spasms. Vision might become slightly blurred. The tremor in your hands can interfere with aircraft control, which is particularly dangerous. Headache can develop. Unlike hypoxia - which creeps up quietly - hyperventilation announces itself loudly. You're acutely aware something's wrong, which is actually helpful for recovery but alarming in the moment. The symptoms can feel like a heart attack or serious medical emergency, which increases anxiety and worsens the hyperventilation. A pilot who doesn't understand hyperventilation might interpret the symptoms as a genuine emergency and panic further. Understanding that these symptoms are reversible and not dangerous is the key to stopping the cycle.
exam_tip: List the symptoms and emphasize that they feel like something serious but aren't dangerous - just uncomfortable and performance-degrading. Knowing this helps you avoid the panic that worsens hyperventilation.
key_facts: Tingling in fingers, lips, and mouth (from alkalosis)
Dizziness, lightheadedness, chest tightness
Palpitations, tremor, possible muscle cramps
Symptoms are obvious and distressing, unlike hypoxia's insidious onset

syllabus_desc: Describe how hyperventilation can be treated.
concept: Hyperventilation treatment is elegant: slow your breathing down. The moment you recognize you're hyperventilating, the fix is to consciously reduce your breathing rate and depth. Your excessive CO₂ loss is reversible - take slower, shallower breaths, and within minutes, your CO₂ levels normalize and symptoms fade. A practical technique is to deliberately take a breath, hold it for a count of 4-5, then exhale slowly. Repeat this 5-10 times. Another approach is to count 'in for 4, hold for 4, out for 4' and stick to that rhythm, resisting the urge to breathe faster. As your symptoms ease (tingling subsides, dizziness clears, chest relaxes), you'll feel a natural inclination to slow your breathing further, which is perfect. The technique works because you're raising your blood CO₂ back to normal levels. The underlying anxiety that triggered the hyperventilation is still there, though, so the cognitive piece matters: recognize that hyperventilation symptoms are not dangerous, they're reversible, and the fix is in your control. That knowledge alone reduces anxiety and makes controlled breathing easier. Some pilots find it helpful to have a mantra: 'I'm hyperventilating, not dying. Slow breathing will fix this.' In a crew environment, your crew-member can help talk you through the breathing technique if you're too panicked to do it alone. Prevention is easier than treatment, though: stress management techniques (regular exercise, mindfulness), good sleep, avoiding caffeine before flights, and building confidence through training all reduce hyperventilation likelihood.
exam_tip: Explain the breathing technique clearly. Emphasize that you're restoring CO₂ balance, not gaining more oxygen - the treatment is conscious breathing control, not gulping air.
key_facts: Treatment: consciously slow breathing rate and depth
Symptoms resolve within minutes of returning to normal breathing
Breathing technique: slow, deliberate breaths (e.g., 4-count in/hold/out)
Understanding that hyperventilation is reversible and not dangerous reduces panic

syllabus_desc: Describe the differences between hyperventilation and hypoxia.
concept: Hyperventilation and hypoxia are sometimes confused because both degrade pilot performance, but they're physiologically distinct and demand opposite responses. Hyperventilation is rapid, excessive breathing causing low blood CO₂; hypoxia is insufficient oxygen in the blood. Hyperventilation is stress-induced and develops acutely (minutes); hypoxia is altitude-induced and develops gradually (can take 30+ minutes at modest altitudes). Hyperventilation symptoms are obvious and alarming - tingling, dizziness, palpitations - and the pilot is acutely aware something's wrong; hypoxia symptoms are subtle and insidious, and the pilot's judgment is so impaired they might not notice or might feel falsely confident. Hyperventilation treatment is slow breathing (reduce ventilation); hypoxia treatment is supplemental oxygen or descent (increase oxygen availability). If a pilot mistakenly treats hyperventilation with supplemental oxygen, it doesn't hurt - oxygen helps a bit - but it doesn't fix the root problem (excess ventilation). If a pilot mistakenly treats hypoxia by slowing their breathing, they're making it worse - reducing ventilation worsens oxygen uptake. In the real world, a pilot might be experiencing both: hypoxic at altitude and anxious enough to hyperventilate. The priority then is to descend (fixing hypoxia) and then manage breathing to prevent or treat hyperventilation. Practically, if you're unsure which you have, descend and use oxygen - that's the safe choice. The distinction matters because it helps you understand your physiology and respond correctly.
exam_tip: Create a comparison table in your head: causes (stress vs. altitude), onset (acute vs. gradual), symptoms (obvious vs. subtle), treatment (slow breathing vs. oxygen/descent). Use this to distinguish them clinically.
key_facts: Hyperventilation: low CO₂ from excessive breathing; hypoxia: low O₂ from altitude
Hyperventilation: acute, obvious, stress-triggered; hypoxia: gradual, insidious, altitude-triggered
Hyperventilation treatment: slow breathing; hypoxia treatment: oxygen or descent
Hyperventilation impairs but doesn't cloud judgment; hypoxia clouds judgment silently
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Hyperventilation and Hypoxia Contrast

syllabus_desc: Define barotrauma.
concept: Barotrauma is damage to body tissues caused by a pressure difference between air spaces inside your body and the surrounding environment. Your body contains air-filled spaces: your lungs, sinuses, middle ear, and GI tract. When external pressure changes (like during altitude changes), the pressure inside these spaces should equalize with the external pressure. If it doesn't, you get barotrauma. The most common barotrauma pilots face is 'sinus squeeze' (sinus barotrauma) or 'ear squeeze' (otitic barotrauma). If you have a head cold and your Eustachian tubes are blocked, descending from altitude creates a pressure imbalance: outside pressure increases, but the air trapped in your middle ear can't escape, so it implodes inward, damaging the delicate tissues. Similarly, if a sinus is blocked by congestion, air inside gets 'trapped' and can't equalize. Lung barotrauma (pulmonary barotrauma or 'the bends') is less common in general aviation but catastrophic: if you hold your breath during a rapid decompression ascent (rare, but possible in an emergency), the air in your lungs expands and can rupture lung tissue. Gastric barotrauma can occur if you have gas in your stomach that can't escape (less common, more relevant to divers). For pilots, the key is understanding that pressure differences create damage, and prevention means ensuring air spaces can equalize freely. Flying with a cold is a genuinely bad idea, not just uncomfortable.
exam_tip: Define it as pressure-related damage to air spaces. Give examples: sinus squeeze from congestion, ear squeeze from a blocked Eustachian tube. Emphasize that it's preventable.
key_facts: Barotrauma: tissue damage from pressure differential in air-filled body spaces
Common types: otitic (ear), sinus, pulmonary (lung)
Occurs when air can't equalize (e.g., blocked Eustachian tubes, sinus congestion)
Prevention: fly healthy, equalize early and often, don't hold your breath

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of barotrauma.
concept: Barotrauma happens when air-filled spaces in your body can't equalize with ambient pressure. The most common cause is flying with congestion - a head cold, sinusitis, allergies, or even just post-nasal drip. Your Eustachian tubes (which connect your middle ear to your throat) are swollen or blocked, trapping air in your middle ear. During descent, outside pressure increases, but the trapped air can't escape, so it compresses, creating a partial vacuum that can rupture blood vessels and damage the delicate tissues. Similarly, if your sinuses are congested, sinus air can't equalize, leading to 'sinus squeeze.' Rapid altitude changes (fast climbs or descents) worsen barotrauma because there's less time for pressure to equalize. Holding your breath is a cause, though rare - if you ascend while holding your breath, the air in your lungs expands; if you then descend rapidly, it contracts. Modern aircraft pressurization reduces barotrauma risk by slowing pressure changes, but unpressurized aircraft mean you're equalizing your body to altitude changes directly. Flying immediately after diving is a cause of barotrauma-like symptoms (though actually decompression sickness, not barotrauma). Decongestants can help open your airways before flight, but the safest approach is simply not flying when congested. Attempting to equalize your ears by holding your nose and blowing (Valsalva maneuver) is the prevention strategy, and if you're unable to equalize because of congestion, you shouldn't fly. It's not worth the damage to your ears.
exam_tip: Focus on congestion as the main preventable cause. Explain the mechanism: blocked tubes → air trapped → pressure imbalance → damage. This makes clear why you don't fly with a cold.
key_facts: Congestion (head cold, sinusitis, allergies) blocks air equalization
Rapid altitude changes reduce time for equalization
Holding your breath or inability to equalize leads to damage
Post-dive flying can cause barotrauma due to residual nitrogen

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of barotrauma.
concept: Barotrauma symptoms depend on which air spaces are affected. Ear barotrauma (the most common) typically starts as discomfort or fullness in the affected ear. If it worsens, you might experience sharp pain, hearing loss, or a sensation of fluid in the ear. In severe cases, you might have vertigo (a spinning sensation), ringing in the ears (tinnitus), or nausea. Blood or fluid might leak from the ear. Sinus barotrauma causes facial pain, particularly around the sinuses (cheeks, forehead, between the eyes). The pain can be sharp or dull, and it typically worsens during descent (when pressure increases). You might have a bloody nose or blood in your saliva. If a sinus or ear is severely damaged, you could have significant bleeding or infection afterward. Gastric barotrauma (gas in the stomach) causes abdominal pain or distension. Pulmonary barotrauma (rare) is serious: chest pain, difficulty breathing, or coughing up blood indicate lung tissue damage, which is a genuine emergency. The key clinical point? Any pain in your ears, sinuses, or chest during altitude changes is a red flag. Don't push through it. If you're experiencing ear or sinus pain, you need to stop that altitude change immediately (usually by descending) and potentially seek medical evaluation. Barotrauma can cause long-term hearing loss or chronic ear problems if repeated or severe, so prevention through not flying when congested is infinitely better than treating the injury.
exam_tip: List symptoms by location. Emphasize that pain during altitude changes is not normal - it's barotrauma starting. You stop the altitude change and descend to relieve pressure.
key_facts: Ear barotrauma: pain, fullness, hearing loss, vertigo, tinnitus
Sinus barotrauma: facial pain, bloody nose
Gastric barotrauma: abdominal pain or distension
Pulmonary barotrauma: chest pain, breathing difficulty (rare, serious)

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of barotrauma on the various parts of the body.
concept: Barotrauma affects different body parts in distinct ways. Your middle ear is particularly vulnerable. The eardrum (tympanum) is a delicate membrane; pressure imbalance causes it to bow inward (during descent when you can't equalize) or outward (during ascent). Repeated stress ruptures the eardrum, causing immediate pain, hearing loss, and infection risk. The small bones of the middle ear (ossicles) can be damaged by the pressure stress. The Eustachian tube itself can be traumatized, leading to long-term dysfunction. Your sinuses contain air surrounded by thin bone; pressure imbalance causes blood vessel rupture, leading to bleeding into the sinus and pain. Severe pressure damage can erode the sinus lining. Your inner ear (labyrinth) is sensitive to pressure changes; damage there causes vertigo, which is dangerous in the cockpit. Your lungs can be damaged if you hold your breath during rapid decompression; expanding air ruptures alveoli and can cause air to escape into your chest cavity (pneumothorax), a serious emergency. Your stomach can distend from trapped gas, causing pain and nausea. Your teeth can even experience barotrauma if there's air trapped under a filling or crown, though this is rare. The severity depends on the magnitude and duration of the pressure differential. A mild sinus squeeze might cause temporary discomfort; a ruptured eardrum is a permanent injury requiring medical intervention. The practical point? Prevent barotrauma by never flying congested, equalize early and often during climbs and descents, and never hold your breath. The consequences of barotrauma can affect your hearing, balance, and ability to fly for months or permanently.
exam_tip: Work through the effects on each body part systematically. Emphasize that damage ranges from mild (temporary pain) to severe (permanent hearing loss, dangerous vertigo in the cockpit).
key_facts: Middle ear: eardrum rupture, ossicle damage, Eustachian tube trauma
Sinuses: blood vessel rupture, bleeding, lining erosion
Inner ear: vertigo from pressure damage
Lungs: alveolar rupture, pneumothorax (if breath held during decompression)

syllabus_desc: Define decompression sickness.
concept: Decompression sickness ('the bends') happens when you're exposed to pressure (typically underwater diving) and then decompress too quickly. At depth, your lungs absorb nitrogen from the air you're breathing, and that nitrogen dissolves into your blood and tissues. If you decompress slowly (ascending gradually with decompression stops), nitrogen comes out of solution gradually and is exhaled normally. But if you ascend too fast, nitrogen comes out of solution faster than your lungs can remove it, forming bubbles in your blood and tissues. These bubbles can block blood vessels (causing pain, paralysis, or worse), irritate nerves, or cause other problems. For pilots, decompression sickness is relevant mainly in the context of flying after diving. You've absorbed nitrogen while diving; if you then fly to altitude too soon, the reduced cabin or cockpit pressure means the nitrogen in your body has an even greater pressure gradient and comes out of solution more readily, causing 'the bends.' Pilots who dive and then fly must wait adequate time for nitrogen to off-gas naturally. The deeper and longer the dive, the more nitrogen absorbed and the longer the required waiting period. For sport diving (typical recreational depths), the safe rule is 12-24 hours before flying unpressurized; some sources recommend waiting until the next day. In a pressurized aircraft at cabin altitude (roughly 8,000 feet equivalent), the risk is lower but still present for aggressive diving. For pilots in New Zealand who enjoy water sports at the Marlborough Sounds or Bay of Islands, this is a real consideration.
exam_tip: Define it clearly: nitrogen bubbles from too-rapid decompression. Explain why it matters to pilots (diving then flying), not just to divers. Memorize the 12-24 hour wait rule.
key_facts: Decompression sickness: nitrogen bubbles form when decompressing too quickly
Nitrogen dissolved at pressure comes out of solution if pressure drops too fast
Bubbles block blood vessels or irritate tissues, causing pain, paralysis, or worse
Pilots who dive must wait 12-24+ hours before flying (depth/duration dependent)
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Decompression Sickness in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of decompression sickness.
concept: Decompression sickness is caused by rapid pressure reduction, particularly after exposure to elevated pressure (diving). The mechanism is straightforward: at depth, your body absorbs more nitrogen than it normally does (Boyle's Law - increased pressure causes more gas to dissolve in blood). The deeper you go and the longer you stay, the more nitrogen is absorbed. If you then ascend too quickly, the pressure drops faster than your body can off-gas nitrogen safely. Nitrogen comes out of solution as bubbles. For pilots, the main cause is flying too soon after diving. You've absorbed significant nitrogen at depth, and then you climb to altitude (or enter a pressurized cabin that's equivalent to high altitude), further reducing pressure and forcing nitrogen out of solution. The risk scales with dive depth and duration: a shallow 20-minute dive has less risk than a 60-minute dive to 30 meters. Multiple dives in one day ('repetitive diving') increases risk. Even if you ascended from your dive slowly and properly, the additional pressure reduction from altitude exposure can still cause decompression sickness. Flying in an unpressurized aircraft to high altitude carries more risk than flying in a pressurized cabin. Rapid decompression (emergency depressurization in flight) creates the same pressure drop and can cause decompression sickness even if you weren't diving. Very few pilots experience decompression sickness from flight alone, but it's theoretically possible. The practical cause for pilots is the combination of diving and flying: don't do it without adequate decompression time in between.
exam_tip: Explain the gas physics: increased pressure → more nitrogen dissolves; decreased pressure → nitrogen bubbles form. This makes clear why diving then flying is risky.
key_facts: Elevated pressure (diving) causes nitrogen absorption in blood and tissues
Rapid pressure reduction causes nitrogen to come out of solution as bubbles
Depth and dive duration determine nitrogen load; longer/deeper dives increase risk
Flying to altitude after diving compounds the problem by further reducing pressure
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Decompression Sickness in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of decompression sickness.
concept: Decompression sickness symptoms vary widely depending on where nitrogen bubbles form and how large they are. The classic symptom is pain - often in joints (knees, shoulders, elbows, wrists), which is why it's called 'the bends.' The pain can be mild or severe; sometimes it's described as a dull ache, sometimes sharp. Bubbles in the nervous system cause neurological symptoms: weakness or paralysis (often in the legs), numbness or tingling, difficulty with coordination. If bubbles affect the lungs ('the chokes'), you might experience chest pain, coughing, or difficulty breathing. Bubbles in the inner ear cause vertigo and hearing loss. Skin symptoms include itching, rashes, or blotchy discoloration ('the creeps'). Some people experience fatigue, dizziness, or malaise. In severe cases, loss of consciousness or death can occur if bubbles are large or in critical locations. The timing of symptom onset varies: some symptoms appear immediately after decompression (within minutes to hours), while others develop over 24-48 hours. This delayed onset is dangerous because a pilot might dive, fly later that day, and then develop symptoms hours later. A pilot experiencing joint pain, weakness, or neurological symptoms after diving and flying should seek immediate medical attention and explain the diving and flying history. Hyperbaric oxygen treatment is the specific therapy - high-pressure oxygen forces bubbles to shrink and resorb. The lesson: if you develop any unusual symptoms after diving and flying, assume it's decompression sickness and seek help.
exam_tip: Describe the range of symptoms and emphasize that decompression sickness is not obviously related to diving (symptoms come later, after flying). If a pilot develops joint pain or weakness after diving/flying, it's decompression sickness until proven otherwise.
key_facts: Joint pain ('the bends') is classic but not always present
Neurological symptoms: weakness, paralysis, numbness, incoordination
Respiratory symptoms: chest pain, coughing, difficulty breathing
Symptoms can be delayed (up to 24-48 hours), making diagnosis tricky
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Decompression Sickness in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Explain how decompression sickness can be prevented.
concept: Decompression sickness prevention for pilots boils down to one principle: don't dive and fly on the same day, or wait adequate time between diving and flying. The industry standard is 12 hours minimum before flying in a pressurized aircraft, and 24 hours before flying unpressurized or climbing to high altitude. Some dive organizations recommend 24 hours as a blanket rule. For aggressive diving (deep dives, multiple dives, or long bottom times), waiting longer is prudent. The rationale is simple: your body needs time to off-gas the nitrogen absorbed during diving. You can't rush this process without creating bubbles. If you're a water sports enthusiast - diving in the Marlborough Sounds, the Bay of Islands, or off the coasts near Auckland or Wellington - you need to plan your flying around your diving, not the reverse. Another prevention strategy is avoiding diving entirely if you're planning to fly that day or the next. If you're a pilot who also dives, you might choose to make weekend diving trips and not fly during them. Flying in a pressurized cabin is safer than flying unpressurized after diving, but the rule still applies: wait 12-24 hours. The absolute prohibition is emergency decompression in flight: if your aircraft suffers a rapid decompression, the risk of decompression sickness is real, though statistically rare. There's no cure other than getting to a hyperbaric chamber; prevention is your only defense. As a pilot and diver, make a decision: if you've dived today, you're not flying today or tomorrow without express clearance from medical professionals familiar with decompression sickness.
exam_tip: Memorize the 12/24 hour rule. Emphasize that you can't rush nitrogen off-gassing - your physiology doesn't change because you're in a hurry.
key_facts: Wait 12 hours before flying pressurized; 24 hours before flying unpressurized after diving
Deeper/longer dives require longer wait times
Multiple dives in one day increase nitrogen load and required wait time
Plan your schedule to separate diving and flying; don't try to rush the process
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Decompression Sickness Prevention in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Describe how decompression sickness can be treated.
concept: Decompression sickness treatment is medical and requires immediate professional help. The specific therapy is hyperbaric oxygen: you're placed in a pressurized chamber and given 100% oxygen at elevated pressure. The elevated pressure forces nitrogen bubbles to shrink and resorb into the bloodstream, where it's eventually exhaled. Simultaneous administration of oxygen helps your body eliminate nitrogen faster. The treatment is administered according to established protocols (U.S. Navy decompression tables) depending on symptom severity and type. Early treatment is critical: the sooner you enter a hyperbaric chamber, the better the outcome. Symptoms treated within hours have better prognosis than those treated days later. In New Zealand, hyperbaric treatment is available in major centers like Auckland, but not in every town. If a pilot develops symptoms after diving and flying in a remote area, evacuation to a hyperbaric center might be necessary - a major complication. In the meantime, initial first aid is supportive: rest, hydration, oxygen (breathing high-oxygen air, if available, can help), and avoiding further pressure exposure. Do not attempt to treat decompression sickness yourself or delay seeking medical help. Many symptoms resemble other conditions, so professional diagnosis is essential. The emphasis on prevention should be clear: treatment is complex, requires specialized equipment and expertise, has a long recovery time, and might not fully reverse all damage. Decompression sickness can cause permanent neurological damage. The far better strategy is simply not diving and flying on the same day - a completely controllable decision.
exam_tip: Describe hyperbaric treatment but emphasize prevention. Explain that treatment requires evacuation to a medical center, takes weeks of recovery, and still might not reverse all damage.
key_facts: Treatment: hyperbaric oxygen (pressurized chamber with 100% O₂)
Early treatment (within hours) has better outcomes
Hyperbaric chambers are available in major NZ centers but not everywhere
Recovery is slow; some damage may be permanent
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Decompression Sickness Management in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Explain the dangers of flying after diving.
concept: Flying after diving creates a double exposure to pressure change that dramatically increases decompression sickness risk. You dive, absorbing nitrogen into your blood and tissues. Before your body has time to naturally off-gas that nitrogen, you climb to altitude or enter an aircraft cabin, further reducing ambient pressure. The pressure gradient that pushes nitrogen out of solution becomes even steeper. Nitrogen bubbles form more readily than if you'd simply stayed at sea level after diving. The depth and duration of your dive directly correlate with risk: a shallow 15-minute dive has minimal risk, while a 60-minute dive to 30 meters creates significant nitrogen load. If you then fly unpressurized to 10,000 feet, you're compounding the danger. Even in a pressurized aircraft at cabin altitude (roughly 8,000 feet equivalent), the risk is present. A pilot who dives on a Friday, plans to fly on Friday afternoon, and doesn't wait adequate time is gambling with their neurological function. The dangers are not theoretical: divers who have ignored the waiting period have developed decompression sickness during flight - weakness, paralysis, or loss of consciousness at altitude with no immediate hyperbaric facility available. The outcome can be fatal or permanently disabling. For NZ pilots who enjoy water sports - and many do, given our stunning coastline - the reality is simple: if you dive one day, you don't fly that day or the next without medical clearance. It's not a guideline; it's a rule enforced by your physiology. The danger is real, and the prevention is trivial: just don't do it. A day on the water diving on Saturday and a flight on Monday is safe. A dive on Friday afternoon and a flight Friday evening is dangerous and foolish.
exam_tip: Explain the mechanism clearly: nitrogen from diving is still in your system when you fly, and altitude reduces pressure further, forcing bubbles to form. Emphasize that prevention is trivially easy - just wait.
key_facts: Diving before flying compounds pressure reduction, increasing decompression sickness risk
Risk scales with dive depth and duration
Even pressurized aircraft cabin altitude (8,000 feet equivalent) poses risk
Symptoms can develop during flight, far from hyperbaric treatment
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Diving and Flying in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Describe the anatomy and physiology of the eye.
concept: Your eye is a remarkably precise optical instrument. Light enters through the cornea (the clear front surface), which does most of the focusing. It then passes through the iris - the colored part that controls how much light enters - and the pupil (the hole in the iris). Behind that is the lens, which fine-tunes focus for near and distant objects, getting more curved when you focus on something close. Light is focused onto the retina, a thin layer of light-sensitive cells lining the back of your eye. The retina converts light into electrical signals and sends them via the optic nerve to your brain. Your retina has two types of light-sensitive cells: rods (sensitive in dim light, no color) and cones (sensitive in bright light, provide color vision). Behind your eye is the vitreous humor, a clear gel that fills the eyeball and maintains its shape. The white part you see is the sclera, a tough outer layer that protects the eye and provides attachment points for the muscles that move your eye. Your eye is constantly adjusting: the pupil changes size to regulate light, the lens changes shape to focus, and the extraocular muscles rotate the eye to aim it. The cornea, lens, and vitreous are all transparent; if any become opaque (cataracts), vision degrades. The whole system is built for high-acuity vision in the center (where cones are concentrated) and broader peripheral awareness. Understanding this anatomy helps you appreciate your eyes' strengths and limitations as a pilot.
exam_tip: Draw the eye anatomy from front to back. Label key structures. Explain the path of light and how each part contributes to vision.
key_facts: Light pathway: cornea → iris/pupil → lens → vitreous → retina → optic nerve → brain
Cornea does most focusing; lens fine-tunes focus
Retina contains rods (dim light) and cones (bright light, color)
Six muscles control eye movement; pupil controls light entry
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Eye structure component: lens
concept: The lens is a transparent, biconvex structure behind the iris, responsible for fine-focusing vision. Unlike the cornea, which does most of the focusing and can't change, the lens changes shape to adjust focus - a process called accommodation. When you look at something distant, ciliary muscles relax and the lens flattens, reducing its focusing power. When you look at something close, those muscles contract and pull on zonular fibers attached to the lens, making it more curved and more powerful at bending light. This flexibility is what lets you shift your focus from distant terrain (during scan) to your instrument panel and back. With age, the lens loses elasticity and accommodation becomes harder - presbyopia. By your 40s and 50s, you might need bifocals or progressives. The lens is also vulnerable to damage: UV exposure can cause cataracts (cloudiness), which progressively impairs vision. That's why proper sunglasses are critical - they protect your lens from UV damage. The lens can also be affected by medications or diseases (diabetes, for example). In the cockpit, when you're alternating between distant visual scan and instrument scan, your lens is working constantly. Understanding that your lens is actively accommodating helps you appreciate fatigue in the eyes during long flights.
exam_tip: Explain accommodation: distance → flatter lens; close → curved lens. Mention presbyopia and why older pilots might need reading glasses for the panel.
key_facts: Lens changes shape to focus at different distances (accommodation)
Ciliary muscles control lens shape via zonular fibers
Accommodation becomes harder with age (presbyopia)
UV exposure damages the lens, leading to cataracts
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Eye structure component: cornea
concept: The cornea is the clear, dome-shaped front surface of your eye. It's the primary refractive element - it does about 70% of the focusing work for your eye. The cornea is curved (more curved in the center, flatter at edges) in a way that's optimized for distant vision; it's permanently set to focus light from infinity. Because it can't change shape (unlike the lens), the cornea's curvature is fixed. If your cornea isn't perfectly shaped, you have refractive error: myopia (nearsightedness), hyperopia (farsightedness), or astigmatism (uneven curvature). These are corrected with glasses or contact lenses to bend light to compensate. The cornea is highly sensitive - it has more nerve endings per square millimeter than your fingertips - which is why even small scratches hurt intensely. It's also the eye's first line of defense against dust, debris, and UV radiation. Pilots need to be careful not to scratch their corneas (don't rub your eyes, especially if you have grit in them; flush with clean water). UV exposure damages the cornea and the underlying lens. Extended UV exposure (bright sun reflecting off water or snow) can cause photokeratitis ('snow blindness'), a painful temporary condition where the cornea's surface cells are damaged. This reinforces why quality sunglasses are essential flying equipment, not an accessory. The cornea is transparent because it's avascular (has no blood vessels); blood vessels would scatter light and impair clarity. If the cornea becomes opaque (from scarring or disease), vision is severely impaired.
exam_tip: Emphasize the cornea's role in focusing and its vulnerability to UV. Explain why quality sunglasses (blocking UV) are essential flying equipment.
key_facts: Cornea does ~70% of eye's focusing; permanently curved for distant vision
Refractive errors (myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism) result from corneal shape
Highly sensitive, vulnerable to scratches and UV damage
Avascular (no blood vessels) to maintain transparency
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Eye structure component: retina
concept: The retina is the light-sensitive tissue lining the back of your eye, and it's where the actual visual processing begins. Light is focused by the cornea and lens onto the retina, where photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) convert light energy into electrical signals. Those signals are processed by multiple layers of neurons in the retina before being sent via the optic nerve to your brain. The retina has about 125 million rods and 6-7 million cones distributed across its surface. The distribution is non-uniform: the central area (fovea) is cone-rich and provides sharp, color vision; the peripheral areas are rod-rich and provide motion detection and low-light vision but less acuity. The retina is incredibly metabolically active - it has one of the highest metabolic rates of any tissue in your body, which is why retinal blood supply is critical and why hypoxia and ischemia (lack of blood flow) damage the retina quickly. The retina is vulnerable to damage: detachment (the retina peeling away from the back of the eye) causes vision loss; diabetes damages retinal blood vessels; high pressure (glaucoma) damages retinal nerve cells. In the cockpit, understanding that your retina is concentrating cones in your central vision and rods in your periphery helps you appreciate the importance of scan technique and why central stare (fixating on one thing) is dangerous - you lose peripheral awareness.
exam_tip: Explain the rod/cone distribution and why this matters to pilots: central vision is detailed but narrow; peripheral vision is broader but less detailed. This drives proper scan technique.
key_facts: Retina: light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye
Contains ~125 million rods (low-light, motion detection) and ~6 million cones (bright light, color)
Fovea (central region): cone-rich, provides sharp vision
Periphery: rod-rich, provides motion detection and broad awareness
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Eye structure component: fovea
concept: The fovea (Latin for 'pit') is a small depression in the retina at the very center of your visual field - about the size of a pinhead, covering about 1 degree of your visual field. It's the region of sharpest vision, where cones are densely packed and rods are entirely absent. When you look directly at something, you're positioning the image on your fovea to get the sharpest possible view. Reading text, identifying distant aircraft, checking instruments - all demand foveal vision because they require detail. The fovea is the 'sweet spot' of your retina, but it's also a liability in the cockpit: when you fixate on one detail (tunnel vision), you're using your fovea but losing peripheral awareness. Your peripheral vision, which uses rods, is great at detecting motion but terrible at detail. The fovea has no rods, so it's useless in dim light - which is why at night you can see dim stars by looking slightly away from them (using your rod-rich periphery) but lose them if you look directly at them (using your foveal cones). This foveal gap is a real limitation for night flying. The fovea is also where most retinal diseases (like age-related macular degeneration) cause the most damage, because it's critical for vision and damage there is immediately noticed. In your scan technique, you're constantly moving your fovea from one area of interest to another: horizon, instruments, distant airspace, nearby traffic. The challenge is balancing foveal detail (what you need for instruments) with peripheral awareness (what you need for traffic and the horizon).
exam_tip: Explain that the fovea is where you get detail but also where tunnel vision happens. In night flying, you see stars better by looking to the side (peripheral rods) than directly (foveal cones).
key_facts: Fovea: tiny central region (~1 degree) of sharpest vision
Cone-rich, rod-free; essential for detail vision
Used for reading, identifying aircraft, scanning instruments
Limitation: no rods, so useless at night; fixation causes tunnel vision
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Physiology for Aviators

syllabus_desc: Eye structure component: optic nerve disc
concept: The optic nerve disc (also called the optic papilla) is where all the nerve fibers from your retina bundle together to form the optic nerve, which carries visual information to your brain. It's about 1.5 mm in diameter. The optic nerve disc is a critical structure, but it's also a weak point: it's where the optic nerve fibers can be damaged (glaucoma causes increased pressure that damages these nerve fibers). The optic nerve disc is also notable for one specific reason relevant to pilots: there are no photoreceptors (rods or cones) on the optic nerve disc itself.
