Having options thought through means you're not making decisions under panic. Do: execute your plan. Fly it precisely. Analyze: while executing, analyze what's happening. Are you still on track? Are conditions as expected? Is your decision still good? Analyze continuously. Review: after the flight, review the decision and execution. What went well? What would you do differently? Review is where learning happens. The strength of FDODAR is the emphasis on facts first. A decision's only as good as the facts it's built on. Wishful thinking won't change facts. The emphasis on options ensures you've always got a backup plan. The continuous analysis means you're alert for changes that might require different decisions. And review ensures you learn. The NZ CAA uses FDODAR across all decision-making training. You'll see it emphasized in check rides and in checking. Instructors expect you to explain decisions using FDODAR. For your Kaikoura or Milford coast work, for your weather decisions, for your fuel planning, you should be thinking FDODAR. Facts first, decision second, options third. That's the NZ approach.
exam_tip: Use FDODAR explicitly when discussing any decision. The examiners expect to see it. Show you understand facts come first, not hopes.
key_facts: Facts: gather accurate information first
Decision: make a clear decision based on facts
Options: identify alternatives and backups
Do: execute precisely according to plan
Analyze: continuously assess whether decision remains good
Review: learn from the decision and execution
NZ CAA standard model for all decision-making
Emphasizes facts-first approach and continuous options
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Decision-Making Training, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Identify specific factors that influence the decision making process.
concept: Decision-making doesn't happen in a vacuum. Tonnes of factors influence whether your decision's going to be good. Understanding these factors means you can compensate for them. Stress and time pressure: high stress and time pressure both degrade decision quality. When you're stressed, your thinking narrows, you make faster decisions but worse ones. When you're rushed, you skip steps. Flying into Milford Sound during tough conditions or managing an emergency will stress you. Expect your decision-making to suffer and use your trained procedures to compensate. Fatigue: tired pilots make poor decisions. You're slower, less analytical, more prone to taking risks. Manage your fatigue. Don't fly when exhausted. Get adequate sleep before your flight. Expect your decision-making to be poorer if you're tired. Emotions: anger, fear, overconfidence - emotions strongly influence decisions. Fear makes you risk-averse, sometimes too risk-averse. Anger makes you impulsive. Overconfidence makes you take unnecessary risks. Notice your emotions and consciously counteract them. Environmental factors: weather, workload, noise, temperature, crowding in the cockpit. All of these influence decision quality. High workload degrades decision-making. Poor visibility increases stress. Noise and distraction interfere with analysis. Manage your environment to the extent you can. Group dynamics: if you're flying with others - passengers, crew - group dynamics influence decisions. Peer pressure can push you toward risky decisions. Authority pressure from demanding passengers can do the same. Be aware of this. Make your own decision based on facts, not based on pressure from others. Your own biases: we've covered hazardous attitudes. You've also got confirmation bias (seeking information that confirms what you already believe), availability bias (over-weighting information that's easy to remember), recency bias (over-weighting recent events). Know your biases. Experience and training: more experience and better training generally improve decisions. But both can also create overconfidence and complacency. A very experienced pilot might have excellent judgment or might have gotten sloppy. Training that's reinforced regularly is better than training that's distant. Information quality: the better your facts, the better your decision. Uncertainty about weather, fuel, position all degrade decision quality. Get the best information you can. Ask for updates. Use all available resources. Regulatory and organizational context: CAR Parts, company policies, ATC instructions - these all frame what decisions are even possible. Understand the rules that apply to your decision. Personal factors: your fitness, your hunger, your anxiety about something else in your life - these all influence decisions. Manage them. Eat properly, sleep properly, fly when you're fit. Time available: sometimes you've got time to work through a decision carefully. Sometimes you don't. How much time you have influences how you approach the decision. In an emergency, you execute trained responses. In a planning situation, you might have hours to work through options.
exam_tip: Identify factors that influence your own decision-making. Self-awareness is what examiners are looking for.
key_facts: Stress and time pressure degrade decision quality
Fatigue significantly impairs judgment
Emotions: fear, anger, overconfidence all influence decisions
Environmental factors: noise, workload, weather affect thinking
Group dynamics and peer pressure can push poor decisions
Personal biases distort information processing
Experience and training can improve or hinder judgment
Information quality determines decision quality
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Human Factors Gap Booklet

syllabus_desc: Explain the setting of personal limitations and decision points.
concept: Personal limitations and decision points are where you deliberately restrict yourself below legal or technical limits to maintain a safety margin. You're not legally required to set them, but good pilots do. Your personal minimums create decision boundaries before you're in actual danger. Decision points are predetermined moments where you've already decided what you'll do - divert, turn back, descend - without agonizing in the moment. Personal minimums are honest self-assessment. You might be technically capable of flying steep terrain in marginal conditions, but you're not equally capable in all conditions. Being tired, flying unfamiliar aircraft, new to a route - these all lower your limits. Adjust your limits accordingly. Documenting personal minimums and decision points, then briefing them before the flight and sticking to them - that discipline keeps you alive. TAIC doesn't criticize pilots for being too conservative.
exam_tip: State specific personal minimums and decision points you've set. Show they're realistic and well-reasoned.
key_facts: Personal minimums below legal limits provide safety buffer
Vary with experience, currency, fatigue, aircraft
Decision points are predetermined; remove agonizing in high workload
Breaking personal minimums is where accident chains often develop
Honest self-assessment, not weakness
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Safety Culture Training, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Outline the dangers of get-home-itis.
concept: Get-home-itis is the hazardous attitude of being so focused on reaching your destination that you take risks you normally wouldn't. Pressure to show up, commitment to passengers, wanting to get home - these create temptation to push margins. Weather deteriorates but you continue hoping it improves. Fuel gets low but you press on instead of diverting. Conditions are marginal but you continue with approach below personal minimums because you've come so far. TAIC reports show it's a pattern everywhere. Get-home-itis usually isn't a single bad decision; it's a series of increasingly marginal decisions building a chain. You're more emotionally invested as you progress, so you keep pushing. The counter is your personal minimums and decision points - predetermined limits that remove emotional pressure. Brief your limits to passengers. Be honest about the pressure you feel and deliberately counteract it using FDODAR.
exam_tip: Discuss a realistic scenario where get-home-itis could tempt you. Show how you'd counter it.
key_facts: Determination to reach destination overriding judgment
Develops through series of increasingly marginal decisions
Common in accident investigations
Countered by predetermined minimums and decision points
Requires honest acknowledgment of emotional pressure
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, TAIC Accident Reports, CAA Safety Culture

topic: 10.50  -  Social Psychology and Flight Deck Management
syllabus_desc: Explain how outside resources such as ATC, engineers and other pilots can contribute to a pilot's management of a flight.
concept: You're not flying alone. ATC is your most consistent resource - they're trained, independent, they've got radar and information you don't. Use them actively. Ask for assistance, request vectors, request weather updates. Over the Southern Alps, over Milford Sound, over congested airspace - ATC's there to help. Engineers have aircraft knowledge and experience with problems. Radio your engineer if you've got a maintenance issue. Other pilots have experience; ask another pilot about unfamiliar situations. Your company's procedures and dispatch exist for accident prevention. Follow them. Your crew: use them as a resource. Delegate, ask for input, listen. Passengers: they often know destinations better than you. Ask them. The best pilots actively use resources; overconfident pilots don't. Using resources isn't weakness; it's professionalism.
exam_tip: Give a specific example where you used a resource and how it helped.
key_facts: ATC: use actively for vectors, traffic, weather
Engineers: ask about aircraft malfunctions
Other pilots: ask for perspective on unfamiliar situations
Company resources: procedures exist for safety
Crew: use crew resource management
Passengers: they know the destination, ask them
Best pilots actively solicit input; overconfident pilots don't
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Safety Culture, ICAO Crew Resource Management

syllabus_desc: Explain the role of human error in aviation accidents.
concept: Human error causes most aviation accidents. That's uncomfortable but actually good news because human error is something you can train to manage. Most accidents involve multiple errors building on each other. You don't plan properly so you're uncertain about fuel. You don't cross-check instruments so you're uncertain about position. Uncertainty piles up and something goes wrong. Three types of error: skill-based errors (automatic responses fail - landing a bit long), rules-based errors (wrong rule applied or rule broken), knowledge-based errors (missing knowledge). Most accidents involve multiple errors. Understanding that human error's normal and inevitable means you stop relying on perfection. Instead, you build systems - checklists, procedures, cross-checks - that catch errors before they matter. You design your flying around the assumption that you will make errors, and you've got safeguards to catch them.
exam_tip: Show understanding that you make errors and you've got systems to catch them.
key_facts: Human error causes most aviation accidents
Skill-based, rules-based, knowledge-based errors all occur
Most accidents involve multiple errors building together
Error management, not elimination, is realistic goal
Systems catch errors automatically
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, TAIC Reports, CAA Human Factors Gap Booklet

syllabus_desc: Explain the degree to which human error can be eliminated.
concept: Human error cannot be eliminated. It's physiological fact. Humans make mistakes. This is actually liberating - you stop aiming for perfection and focus on being resilient. Build systems that work even though you'll make mistakes. Rather than aiming for zero errors (impossible), the emphasis is on threat and error management - recognizing errors as they develop and catching them before they cascade. Skilled pilots make errors regularly. The difference is they have systems in place that catch errors. A checklist catches the forgotten item. Cross-checks catch misread instruments. Altitude alert catches the overshoot. These systems don't prevent the error; they catch it. Some errors can't be caught; those sometimes cause accidents. Understanding human error's inevitable should make you humble and rigorous about procedures and checklists.
exam_tip: Explain what systems catch errors in your flying.
key_facts: Human error cannot be eliminated
Errors can be reduced but not prevented
Catch errors before they cascade
Checklists, cross-checks, procedures catch errors
Some errors aren't catchable; those sometimes cause accidents
Humility about error-prone nature improves safety
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Reason Model of Accident Causation, CAA Human Factors

syllabus_desc: Describe the main types of threats which could potentially affect a safe flight.
concept: Threats are things beyond your control that could compromise safety. Weather: thunderstorms, wind shear, icing, low visibility. Plan around it, avoid it, delay for it. Mechanical failures: engines, hydraulics, electrical systems. Maintain and inspect properly, train for failures. Traffic and airspace: maintain awareness, follow ATC, use collision avoidance. Terrain and obstacles: plan routing, avoid in poor visibility. Navigation hazards: use multiple means, cross-check, ask ATC. Health and physiological: maintain fitness, manage fatigue. Fuel limits: plan reserves, divert if necessary. Runway conditions: get current information. Most threats are manageable through planning and awareness.
exam_tip: Identify threats specific to a flight you plan.
key_facts: Weather: cannot control, can plan and avoid
Mechanical: cannot prevent all, can maintain and train
Traffic: cannot control others, can maintain situational awareness
Terrain: cannot move, can plan routing
Navigation: cannot eliminate uncertainty, can cross-check
Health: can manage through fitness
Fuel: can plan reserves
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Threat and Error Management Training

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic elements and features of the Reason Model (Swiss cheese model).
concept: The Reason Model visualizes accidents as multiple failures lining up. Picture slices of Swiss cheese stacked on top of each other. Each slice has holes representing failures or errors. An accident happens when the holes in all slices line up. Usually they don't. Someone makes an error but a checklist catches it. Someone misses an instrument reading but cross-checks catch it. Threats appear but procedures mitigate them. Each slice represents a layer of defense. Modern aircraft have dozens: procedures, checklists, automation, training, crew resource management, terrain awareness, weather radar, traffic collision avoidance. Most errors are caught before they matter. The model emphasizes that accidents need multiple failures aligned - rare but possible. Improvements add layers and reduce hole size, don't eliminate risk. Accidents usually involve multiple contributing factors. System design tolerates human error rather than expecting perfection.
exam_tip: Apply the Reason Model to a realistic accident scenario.
key_facts: Multiple failures must align for accident
Most errors are caught before cascading
Modern aircraft have many defense layers
Improvements add layers, reduce hole size
System design tolerates error
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Safety Management Systems, Reason Model Literature

syllabus_desc: Describe threat management: means of recognising threats.
concept: Threat recognition starts with systematic information gathering. Continuously get weather forecasts, en route updates, traffic calls, instrument indications, external observations. Create habit of asking: what's the actual weather now? What's the wind? What's ahead? Cross-reference information because one source can be wrong. Pattern recognition: know what normal looks like; deviations are threat indicators. Early warning systems: stall warning, terrain awareness, traffic warning - use actively. Ask others: ATC sees beyond your horizon. Use your experience and knowledge of threat patterns. Note deviations from plan - these are threat indicators. Listen to gut feeling; your subconscious picks up patterns. Active threat recognition is discipline and knowledge combined.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd recognize specific threats on a planned flight.
key_facts: Systematic gathering: actively collect data
Cross-reference: don't trust single sources
Pattern recognition: notice deviations from normal
Early warning systems: use actively
Ask others: ATC, pilots, ground
Personal experience: recognize patterns
Gut feeling deserves attention
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Threat and Error Management Training

syllabus_desc: Describe threat management: means of avoiding threats.
concept: Once you've recognized a threat, best management is often avoidance. Route around thunderstorms. Stay out of icing conditions. Avoid wind shear areas. Plan terrain-safe routing in poor visibility. Plan adequate fuel and reserves to reduce fuel threats. Maintain aircraft to prevent mechanical threats. Follow separation procedures to avoid traffic threats. Get adequate sleep to avoid fatigue threats. Match crew to task capability. Stay current and trained. Follow procedures rather than improvising. Good threat avoidance starts with good planning. You identify threats early in planning and design your flight to avoid them. Flying the West Coast in winter, icing is a threat - plan altitude and routing to stay out of it. Flying Kaikoura in summer afternoon, thermal and wind shear threats exist - plan to arrive early.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd plan your flight to avoid identified threats.
key_facts: Route around weather
Plan terrain-safe routing in poor visibility
Plan adequate fuel reserves
Maintain and inspect aircraft
Follow separation procedures
Manage fatigue through sleep
Match crew to task
Stay current and trained
Plan to avoid threats rather than manage mid-flight
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Threat and Error Management Training, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe threat management: means of mitigating the effects of threats.
concept: Sometimes despite planning, a threat develops. When avoidance isn't possible, you mitigate - reduce the impact and manage the situation. Follow emergency procedures for declared emergencies. Divert to an alternate aerodrome. Request assistance from ATC for vectors around weather, radar guidance, holding. Use your aircraft's systems: terrain awareness, traffic collision avoidance, weather radar. Slow down to have more thinking time and options. Climb or descend to gain options and reduce threats. Ask for weather updates from other aircraft. Turn back to where you came from - it's always an option. Declare medical emergency to get priority and help. Personal minimums and decision points mitigate by removing agonizing. Checklists and procedures mitigate errors. Cross-checks mitigate misreading. The principle is: I can't eliminate the threat, but I've got ways to manage it so it doesn't become catastrophic.
exam_tip: Walk through how you'd mitigate a specific threat if avoidance failed.
key_facts: Emergency procedures: trained responses to manage crisis
Diversion: go to alternate when threatened
Request assistance: ATC can help
Use aircraft systems: terrain, traffic, weather
Slow down: buy time to think
Climb/descend: buy options
Ask for information: other pilots' reports
Turn back: always an option
Declare emergency: get priority
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Threat and Error Management Training, Emergency Procedures

syllabus_desc: Identify the elements in a safety culture.
concept: Safety culture is shared beliefs, values, and behaviors about safety within an organization. It's bigger than rules - it's about how people think about safety. Elements: shared commitment to safety from top to bottom (management prioritizes safety over schedule); open communication (people can talk about safety issues without fear); blame-free analysis of incidents (investigate to understand, not to blame); continuous improvement (constantly looking for ways to improve); learning from near-misses (not just accidents); emphasis on systems not just individuals (what in the system allowed this?); feedback loops (people hear when action occurred); senior leadership visibility in safety; transparency about safety data; regular training and awareness; respect for expertise. Organizations with strong safety cultures have fewer accidents. Not because rules are different, but because people genuinely care about doing things safely.
exam_tip: Describe safety culture elements you see in your training.
key_facts: Shared commitment to safety at all levels
Open communication about safety issues
Blame-free investigation of incidents
Continuous improvement focus
Learning from near-misses
Emphasis on systems, not individual blame
Feedback loops and transparency
Regular training and awareness
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Safety Culture Framework, CAR Part 12

syllabus_desc: List the key reasons for safety reporting in aviation.
concept: Safety reporting is foundational to improvement. Reasons: learning and improvement (if something goes wrong and nobody reports, we don't learn); identification of systemic issues (patterns in reports show system problems, not random bad luck); prevention of accidents (near-misses teach us how to prevent actual catastrophes); feedback to organizations (they need to know what's happening); verification of effectiveness (does our new procedure actually work?); incentive for continuous improvement (organizations that act on reports show reporting makes a difference); contribution to national safety (individual reports inform CAA policy for everyone). Some reporting is mandatory (CAR Part 12 requires certain incidents). Discretionary reporting beyond mandatory is encouraged. You see something that might be a safety issue, report it. Make a mistake, report it. See another pilot doing something unsafe, report it. Safety reporting isn't about getting people in trouble; it's about learning and improving.
exam_tip: Explain how you'd report a safety issue and why it improves safety.
key_facts: Learning and improvement from incidents
Identifying systemic issues through patterns
Prevention of accidents through near-miss learning
Feedback to organizations about operations
Verification of safety measure effectiveness
Incentive for continuous improvement
Contributing to national safety knowledge
Mandatory and discretionary reporting both important
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 12, CAA Safety Culture Framework

syllabus_desc: Explain the rationale for mandatory reporting of incidents as required by CAR Part 12.
concept: CAR Part 12 requires reporting of certain incidents and accidents. Rationale: not all safety-critical information gets reported voluntarily. Some incidents might be embarrassing or result in disciplinary action if known. Without mandatory reporting, incidents stay hidden and system learns nothing. CAR Part 12 requires accidents, serious incidents, and certain incidents. There's a threshold of severity, risk, or type. Reports go to CAA and operator within defined time. Intent: get information into system quickly so CAA sees trends. Applies to everyone - commercial and general aviation. CAR Part 12 protects reporters: reports go to Safety Investigation branch, not enforcement. Promise: you won't be punished for honestly reporting mistakes. Your responsibility: know what CAR Part 12 requires you to report, and report it. It's your contribution to the safety system.
exam_tip: Identify a reportable incident scenario and explain why it must be reported to CAA.
key_facts: Mandatory reporting ensures systemic information reaches CAA
Not all incidents reported voluntarily
Defines reportable incidents: accidents, serious incidents, certain incidents
Reports go to Safety Investigation, not enforcement
Honest mistake reporting protected from discipline
Identifies systemic issues through trend analysis
source: CAR Part 12, AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Safety Management Systems

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between normal error, at risk behaviour and high culpability behaviour.
concept: When something goes wrong, investigations distinguish between different types of behavior. Normal error: mistakes that competent people might make. You prepared properly, trained, understand what you're doing, but something went wrong. Misread instrument, misheard radio call, missed something despite following checklist. Response: learning, improve training, improve procedures. No punishment. At-risk behavior: you're taking a shortcut outside rules but you think you're safe. Skip a step because you've done it a thousand times. Push minimums slightly. Don't do full preflight because you're rushing. You're aware you're outside normal but believe you're safe. Response: examine your thinking, additional training or counseling. Understanding that your shortcut increased risk. High culpability: you're knowingly doing something unsafe. Flying while knowing you're too fatigued. Flying into weather knowing you shouldn't. Deliberately circumventing critical safety. Not from ignorance; deliberately. Response: discipline. Safety culture depends on enforcing against high culpability. The framework shapes investigation responses: normal error gets learning, at-risk gets training, high culpability gets discipline.
exam_tip: Classify a scenario incident as normal error, at-risk, or high culpability.
key_facts: Normal error: doing right thing, something went wrong anyway
At-risk behavior: deliberate shortcut, think it's safe
High culpability: knowing violation, deliberately unsafe
Distinction based on mental state and awareness
Normal error expected; at-risk and high culpability are not
Responses vary: learning, training, discipline
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 12, CAA Safety Culture Framework

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between negligent and reckless behaviour.
concept: Negligent behavior: breach of duty through insufficient care. You weren't intending harm but you weren't careful enough. Failed to maintain aircraft, failed to follow procedures, failed to maintain fitness. Civil liability possible - damages you pay. Regulatory action possible - CAA suspends license. Reckless behavior: aware of risk and deliberately disregard it. Know something's dangerous and do it anyway. Know you're unfit to fly and fly anyway. Know aircraft is unserviceable and fly it. Deliberately and consciously take unjustifiable risk. More culpable than negligence. Can result in criminal charges - prosecution for reckless endangerment. Significantly more serious. Distinction matters legally and ethically. Negligence is breach of duty through insufficient care. Recklessness is deliberately disregarding known risk. Both serious, both have consequences. Recklessness involves knowledge and deliberate choice making it more culpable.
exam_tip: Distinguish negligent from reckless behavior in a scenario.
key_facts: Negligence: breach of duty through insufficient care
Recklessness: knowing and deliberate disregard of known risk
Negligence results from not being careful enough
Recklessness from deliberately ignoring danger
Both have regulatory consequences
Recklessness more culpable, potentially criminal
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 12, NZ Legal Framework

syllabus_desc: Describe the role of punitive sanction.
concept: Punitive sanction is discipline - consequences for violating rules. Role: deter reckless or deliberately unsafe behavior. If people know recklessness results in consequences - license suspension, termination, prosecution - they're deterred. Without consequences, some people would disregard safety. Sanctions maintain standards and enforce that safety is mandatory. But sanctions aren't the primary safety improvement mechanism. Punish every error, including normal errors, you create culture where people hide errors. System learns nothing. Applied carefully: punish high culpability behavior deliberately and severely enough to deter. Don't punish normal errors - learn from them. Balance is crucial: sanction should deter behaviors most dangerous to aviation. Deliberate rule violation, recklessness, gross negligence are dangerous. Normal errors managed through training and system improvement. Sanctions must be consistent and fair or system loses credibility. Primary tools of safety management: training, procedures, systems design, learning from incidents, building culture where safety is valued. Sanctions enforce the boundary: if you deliberately cross into recklessness, you face consequences.
exam_tip: Explain how punitive sanctions support safety without degrading culture.
key_facts: Punitive sanctions deter reckless and deliberately unsafe behavior
But: overuse hides errors and degrades learning
Applied to high culpability, not normal errors
Balance: severe sanctions for recklessness, learning for normal errors
Consistency and fairness crucial
Sanctions support culture, not primary mechanism
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAA Safety Culture Framework, CAR Part 12

syllabus_desc: Describe how information can be presented using display instruments.
concept: Instruments convey information. Design matters because poor design confuses or misleads. Analog displays: round instruments with needles. Perceive altitude as needle position relative to markings. Give good situation awareness - see trending up, level, down. Disadvantage: precision harder than digital. Digital displays: numeric readout of exact values. Advantage: precision. Disadvantage: lose trending. Glass cockpits combine both. Tape displays: numbers scroll vertically showing past, current, future values - useful for trending. Gauge vs scale: scale requires interpretation; gauge showing green (good) and red (bad) more intuitive. Units must be clear: feet or meters, knots or mph? Ambiguous units cause errors. Scaling must be rational. Positioning matters: essential instruments in primary field of view. Grouping: related instruments together reduce scanning and increase efficiency. Color coding: green normal, yellow caution, red warning. Consistent use helps rapid interpretation. Redundancy: critical parameters have backups. Principle: good display makes correct interpretation easy and incorrect interpretation difficult.
exam_tip: Discuss how a specific instrument display conveys information.
key_facts: Analog shows trending; digital shows precision
Glass cockpits combine both
Clear units prevent ambiguity
Logical scaling makes numbers intuitive
Critical instruments in primary field of view
Grouping related instruments reduces scanning
Color coding: green/yellow/red for status
Redundancy: critical parameters have backups

syllabus_desc: Explain the limitations of alerting systems.
concept: Alerting systems warn about problems: warning lights, aural alerts, terrain awareness, traffic collision avoidance. Safety tools with limitations. Alert fatigue: frequent or false alerts, you ignore them. You hear alert and first response is 'is this real?' rather than 'immediate action required.' Pilots learn to ignore alerts; actual critical alerts get ignored. Threshold setting: set threshold too high, no alerts when you should get them. Too low, nuisance alerts. Getting right threshold is hard. Lack of context: alert tells you something's wrong, not always why or what to do. Interpret within context. Delayed alerts: some issues develop gradually; alert comes only when threshold reached. Competing alerts: multiple simultaneous alerts compete for attention. You might address the wrong one first. Automation surprise: automated system fails but alert comes late. Reliance on alerts: rely entirely on alerts, stop actively monitoring. Some problems develop without triggering alerts. Not all problems have alerts: some aircraft systems don't. Some problems develop without automatic alerts. Limitation means: don't become dependent on alerts. Use them, maintain active monitoring. Verify alert against other information. Over time, you learn which alerts are reliable and which are nuisance.
exam_tip: Discuss how you'd respond to an alert while cross-checking.
key_facts: Alert fatigue from frequent/false alerts
Threshold setting: wrong threshold causes missed or false alerts
Lack of context: alert doesn't always tell you why
Delayed alerts: some issues develop before alert triggers
Competing alerts: multiple simultaneous
Some problems don't have alerts
Don't become dependent on alerting systems
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Human Factors in Aircraft Operations

syllabus_desc: Explain common types of instrument reading errors.
concept: You look at an instrument and misinterpret. Fixation: focus on one instrument, ignore others. Watching heading, don't notice altitude drifting. Omission: skip looking at an instrument entirely. Miss fuel gauge, don't notice fuel is low until critical. Reversal: misread direction or polarity. Vertical speed shows descent, you read as ascending. Misinterpreting scale: altitude indicators with small numbers, fuel gauges with ambiguous markings. Confusing similar instruments: check altitude thinking you're checking heading. Parallax error: looking at angle instead of straight-on gives inaccurate reading. Context-dependent misreading: expect instrument to show one thing, read it that way even though it's different. Expectation bias affects reading. Decimal point errors: read 2500 as 250. Instrument failure: don't realize instrument gives false information. Minimize errors: systematic cross-checking. Don't rely on single instrument. Verify altitude with multiple altimeters. Cross-check reduces errors because one wrong instrument is caught. Use instrument scan - maintain disciplined scan, don't fixate. Understand instruments specifically. Know scales, units. Know what's normal for each flight condition. Be skeptical - if something seems wrong, check multiple ways.
exam_tip: Identify a specific reading error and explain how cross-checking catches it.
key_facts: Fixation: focusing on one, missing others
Omission: skipping an instrument in scanning
Reversal: misreading direction
Context-dependent errors: expectation biasing reading
Decimal point errors: misplacing point
Malfunction: instrument providing false information
Cross-checking: best defense

syllabus_desc: Explain how instrument interpretation errors can be minimised.
concept: Minimize through discipline and systems. Proper scanning technique: systematic scan pattern, not random. Primary instruments for flight mode, secondary for trim and control. Scan every instrument in sequence. Discipline of regular scanning prevents fixation and omission. Cross-checking: don't rely on single instruments. Verify heading using heading indicator, magnetic compass, and tracking. Verify altitude using altimeter, vertical speed, and attitude. Verify airspeed using airspeed indicator, pitch and power. Cross-checks catch errors and malfunctions. Instrument preflight and familiarization: before flight, verify instruments working correctly. Know how they work. Know what values should show in different conditions. Checklist discipline: checklists often include verification steps. Before takeoff, verify altimeter set correctly. Before approach, verify all instruments functioning. Understanding what's normal: know what instruments should show in different flight conditions. Knowing normal helps spot abnormal. Recognize instrument failure: know what failed instrument looks like. Unusual values, unresponsive, contradicts other instruments. Partial panel procedures: if instrument fails, practice flying without it. Slowing down: high workload increases error. If confused, slow down, reduce workload. Communication: if uncertain about instrument reading, ask. Ask ATC what they're seeing. External perspective catches misinterpretations.
exam_tip: Explain systematic approach to instrument interpretation.
key_facts: Systematic scanning prevents fixation
Cross-checking catches errors and malfunctions
Preflight familiarization prevents surprises
Checklist discipline catches problems early
Knowing normal helps spot abnormal
Recognizing instrument failure critical
Partial panel training improves interpretation
Slowing down reduces errors
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Instrument Flying Handbook, Glass Cockpit Training

syllabus_desc: Describe the concepts of an ergonomically designed cockpit.
concept: Ergonomic design arranges cockpit so you operate efficiently, safely, without unnecessary strain or error. Good design reduces workload and errors. Reach and visibility: controls and instruments within comfortable reach, visible from normal seating without extreme head movement. Poor reach forces awkward positions and increases errors. Primary instruments visible: critical info - altitude, heading, airspeed - in primary field of view. Instrument grouping: related instruments grouped together reduce scanning. Engine instruments together, flight instruments together, navigation together. Standardization: standard layout (standard six-pack arrangement) makes transition to different aircraft easier. Controls positioned logically: throttle, mixture, prop pitch in logical sequence. Landing gear, flap controls obvious, accessed deliberately. Control differentiation: different controls feel different so you don't grab the wrong one. Gear lever might have wheel grip, flap lever flap-shaped grip. Labeling: clear labels for every control and instrument. Good lighting: instruments and controls well-lit and readable. Seat adjustability: seats adjust to different pilot sizes so everyone reaches and sees. Noise management: cockpit noise manageable so you hear alarms and communicate. Vibration isolation: vibration minimized so instruments stable and readable. Workspace organization: storage, cup holders, areas for charts organized so items accessible, not cluttering. Emergency equipment placement: easy to find and access. Accessibility for different body sizes: accommodate range of pilot sizes and capabilities. Control feel: controls give feedback so you know they're responding. Overall: reducing unnecessary strain, reducing errors, making operation efficient.
exam_tip: Discuss ergonomic features you appreciate in your training aircraft.
key_facts: Reach and visibility: controls and instruments within easy reach
Primary instruments in primary field of view
Grouping: related instruments together
Standardization: matches standard across types
Logical control position: follows natural sequence
Differentiation: different controls feel different
Clear labeling: no ambiguity
Adequate lighting: readable but glare-free
Adjustability: accommodates range of pilot sizes
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Cockpit Design Standards, Human Factors in Aviation

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose and rationale behind consistent and thorough checklist use.
concept: Checklists are memory aids and discipline tools. Purpose: ensure all required items completed before proceeding. Rationale: memory is fallible; checklists offload memory burden onto external document. Without checklists, items get forgotten, especially under stress or high workload. Checklists standardize procedures across all pilots so everyone does things the same way. Reduces variation and reduces errors from doing things differently. Checklists force deliberation: items aren't just done absent-mindedly; they're executed and verified. This deliberate action catches problems early. Checklists create discipline - pilots develop habit of systematic completion. This discipline transfers to other tasks. Checklists serve as training tool for new pilots - they learn proper sequence and what items are critical. Checklists serve as troubleshooting tool when something goes wrong - emergency checklists guide response to problems. Thorough checklist use means actually executing items, not just reading words. Item is checked when you've done the item and verified correct. That verification step is critical. Sloppy checklist use - reading without doing - defeats the purpose. Proper use: read item, execute action, verify result. This process catches errors and ensures quality. Thorough use prevents errors, catches problems early, and builds discipline. It's not time-wasted; it's time saved by preventing problems and catching errors before they matter.
exam_tip: Explain why thorough checklist use improves safety, not just procedure compliance.
key_facts: Checklists offload memory burden to external document
Standardize procedures across all pilots
Force deliberate execution and verification
Build discipline and systematic habit
Serve as training and troubleshooting tools
Thorough use means executing and verifying, not just reading
Prevent errors and catch problems early
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91 SOPs, Document and Procedure Standards

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of normal checklists.
concept: Normal checklists are procedures for routine operations: preflight, before takeoff, before landing, cruise checks. They're preventative - designed to catch problems before they matter, to ensure aircraft and crew are ready for each phase. Before preflight: external walk-around, looking for damage, contamination, security. Before takeoff: fuel, trim, instruments, flight controls, electrical, engine parameters. Before landing: fuel, descent planning, landing distance available, weather, runway condition. Cruise: periodic scan of engine parameters, aircraft systems, navigation, fuel consumption. Normal checklists follow aircraft type procedures and operator SOPs. Execution method varies: some read-do (you read item, you execute), some read-do-verify (read, execute, verify correct), some challenge-response (one pilot challenges, other responds). The method should be deliberate, not rushed. Items should be executed completely before moving to next item. Crew coordination: if two crew, one operates, one monitors and verifies. Items should be completed with deliberation; rushing increases error rate. Normal checklists aren't emergency procedures; they're routine procedures for regular operations. They're done frequently so crew knows them well.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd execute a normal checklist with deliberation and verification.
key_facts: Preflight, before takeoff, before landing, cruise checks
Preventative: catch problems before they matter
Follow aircraft type procedures and operator SOPs
Deliberate execution, not rushed
Crew coordination: one operates, one verifies
Completed completely before moving to next item
Familiar procedures done frequently
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91 Standard Operating Procedures

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of emergency checklists.
concept: Emergency checklists are procedures for abnormal situations: engine fire, engine failure, electrical failure, hydraulic loss, pressurization loss, unusual attitudes, emergency descent. They guide response to problems. Purpose: when something goes wrong, immediate action items are prioritized. Some items are critical immediate actions (fire demands immediate action); other items are secondary (once the immediate crisis is managed). Emergency checklists are structured to get you stabilized first, then work toward a solution. Example: engine fire checklist: immediate action is isolate electrical power to that engine, close fuel valve, activate fire extinguisher. Secondary items are check temperature, monitor other engines. Execution is fast: you're not leisurely working through checklist. You execute and move on. Crew coordination: one pilot flies, other executes checklist. Or pilot does critical immediate actions, then works checklist. Training for emergency checklists is critical because you don't have time to read and think. You need to know procedures well enough to execute by memory, then verify with checklist. Emergency checklists exist for scenarios you've trained for. Truly novel emergencies might not have procedures. Your basic airmanship and training guide you then.
exam_tip: Discuss how you'd execute an emergency checklist under real pressure.
key_facts: Procedures for abnormal situations: fires, failures, unusual attitudes
Structured: immediate critical actions first, then secondary
Executed fast, not leisurely
Crew coordination: one flies, one executes
Requires training so you know procedures
Execute by memory, verify with checklist
Exist for scenarios you've trained for
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Emergency Procedures Manual, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe situations where checklists alone are not enough.
concept: Checklists are tools, but they have limitations. Situation: problem not on checklist. Aircraft has malfunction that doesn't match any emergency procedure. You've got to understand principles of aircraft operation and apply them. Situation: checklist assumes normal environment. You're executing emergency descent checklist assuming normal functioning of basic systems. But what if multiple systems are failing? What if the problem is worse than the checklist assumes? Situation: time pressure so severe you can't execute checklist. You've got immediate actions you must take before you even get to checklist. Your training must prepare you for that. Situation: crew incapacitation. One pilot is unable to assist; other pilot must fly and execute emergency procedures. Single pilot can't do everything; priorities must be chosen. Situation: decisions require judgment beyond checklist. Checklist tells you what to do for engine failure. But engine failure where? Over mountains or over water? Choices might be different. Situation: unfamiliar aircraft with slightly different procedures. Checklist from your usual aircraft might not apply. Situation: checklist error. Written incorrectly or outdated. You have to recognize the error and work around it. Situation: novel problem requiring creative thinking. Checklists are tools within broader training and judgment. Training in principles, in basic airmanship, in problem-solving - these are what you rely on when checklists don't cover the situation. Checklists are invaluable within their scope, but they're not a substitute for understanding and judgment.
exam_tip: Describe a scenario where checklist isn't adequate and explain what you'd do.
key_facts: Problem not on existing checklist
Checklist assumes normal environment; reality more complex
Time pressure prevents full checklist execution
Crew incapacitation limits checklist execution
Decisions require judgment beyond procedure
Novel problems require creative thinking
Training in principles and judgment required
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Emergency Procedures, Human Factors Training

syllabus_desc: Identify typical errors associated with the use of checklists (checklist complacency).
concept: Checklist complacency is when you've used a checklist so many times that you stop actually paying attention. Errors: reading without doing. You read items aloud but you don't execute them. You're just going through the motions. Item gets missed or done incorrectly. Skipping items you've done a thousand times. 'I've retracted the gear a thousand times without a problem, I don't need to check it this time.' Exactly when you don't check is when something goes wrong. Shortcutting the verification step. Item says 'verify landing gear down.' Instead of actually verifying (looking, feeling, checking indicator), you just assume it's down. Doing items out of sequence because you know the checklist and you're rushing. Sequence matters sometimes; doing things in wrong order can miss dependencies. Not catching problems because you expect everything to be fine. You read 'oil temperature' and see a gauge reading that's slightly high, but you're not really paying attention, so you miss it. Not documenting or reporting problems found during checklist because you're rushing. Finding an inop item and just moving on instead of reporting it. Allowing distractions to interrupt checklist execution. Someone talks to you, you miss where you were, you restart without knowing. Counter: treat every checklist as important. Even if you've done it a thousand times, this time might be different. Deliberately execute and verify each item. Don't shortcut. If an item seems done already, verify it again. Discipline yourself to complete checklist even when you're in a hurry. Interruptions should pause checklist; restart properly. Complacency is dangerous because it's subtle - you feel like you're doing proper procedure while actually cutting corners. Awareness of complacency tendency helps. Some operators use discipline items or callouts specifically to fight complacency. The answer to complacency is: treat every flight and every checklist as though it matters. Because it does.
exam_tip: Identify a checklist complacency error and explain how to prevent it.
key_facts: Reading without doing items
Skipping familiar items
Skipping or shortcutting verification
Doing items out of sequence
Not catching problems because attention is lax
Not reporting items found
Subtle and dangerous
Counter: deliberate execution even when familiar
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, TAIC Reports, Checklist Discipline Training

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose of SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures).
concept: Standard Operating Procedures are the documented way your company or operation expects you to do things. They're procedures that apply to all operations, all crew. Purpose: standardization. If every pilot does things slightly differently, consistency breaks down. When everyone does things the same way, crew can work together effectively (crew resource management requires understanding how your crew operates). Safety: procedures are designed around safety. They're typically based on best practice, accident investigation learning, and regulatory requirements. Emergency procedures: SOPs specify how to respond to problems. Consistency: if you fly with different crew, you know they're operating under same procedures. If you're suddenly incapacitated and another pilot takes over, they know your procedures. Training: new crew are trained on SOPs. Everyone flying for your company knows what's expected. Quality: procedures define standards. Deviations from SOP need approval. If you think you need to deviate, that's noted and analyzed. Compliance: CAR Part 91 and CAR Part 135 (commercial) require SOPs. Part 135 specifies procedures; Part 91 gives operators responsibility to establish procedures. Consistency across the operation: everyone's doing things the same way. Your responsibility as a pilot: know your SOPs. Follow them unless there's a legitimate reason to deviate. If you deviate, document it and report it. Work toward improving SOPs if they have problems. SOPs exist for your safety and the safety of everyone on the flight.
exam_tip: Explain how you'd follow your company SOPs and what you'd do if an SOP seemed wrong.
key_facts: Standardization: everyone does things the same way
Safety: designed around safety principles
Emergency procedures specified
Training: new crew learn procedures
Quality: standards defined and monitored
Regulatory: required by CAR Parts
Consistency: crew can work together effectively
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91 and Part 135, SOP Standards

syllabus_desc: Describe first aid treatment for shock.
concept: Shock is dangerous medical state where blood pressure drops and tissues aren't getting adequate oxygen. Signs: pale, clammy, rapid weak pulse, rapid breathing, weakness, confusion, nausea. First aid: lay person flat, elevate legs (unless head or spinal injury suspected), keep warm with blankets or clothing (shock involves loss of body temperature). Keep person calm; reassurance helps. Loosen tight clothing. Don't give food or water - might vomit or cause aspiration if unconscious. Monitor breathing and pulse. If unconscious and breathing, recovery position (on side). If not breathing, CPR. Oxygen if available. Get medical help quickly. Don't leave person alone. In aircraft, lay them flat in cabin if possible. Stop flight if person is significantly incapacitated. Get on ground and get them to medical facility. Shock can be life-threatening if not managed. Prevention of shock: identify and treat cause (bleeding, severe pain, etc.). Control bleeding, manage injuries, minimize pain. Oxygen helps if available. Recognition of shock early and intervention improves outcomes.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd recognize and treat shock in an aircraft emergency.
key_facts: Shock: pale, clammy, rapid weak pulse, rapid breathing
Lay flat, elevate legs unless spinal injury
Keep warm, keep calm
No food or water
Monitor breathing and pulse
Get medical help quickly
Prevention: treat cause, control bleeding, manage pain
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, First Aid Training, First Aid Manuals

syllabus_desc: Describe first aid treatment for burns.
concept: Burns can range from minor to life-threatening. First aid depends on severity. Minor burns (redness, mild pain): cool water for 10-20 minutes to stop burning. Don't use ice directly (can damage tissue). Cover with clean cloth. Over-the-counter burn cream if available. Moderate burns (blistering, more extensive): cool water. Don't apply ice directly. Don't pop blisters. Cover loosely with clean cloth or sterile dressing. Pain management: paracetamol if conscious and can swallow. Elevate burned area if possible. Get medical attention soon. Severe burns (deep tissue damage, large areas, burns on face, hands, genitals): cool water initially but don't use ice. Cover with clean dry cloth - don't apply creams or ointments. Remove jewelry or tight clothing if possible and area isn't already swollen. If clothing is stuck, don't remove it. Pain management. Get emergency medical help urgently. Don't give food or water by mouth (might need surgery). Prevention in aircraft: use caution with coffee, hot food, ovens. Keep fire suppression equipment available. Know how to use it. In case of fire, shut down electrical, use suppression equipment. If serious burns occur in flight (cabin fire or crew injured), land immediately. Declare emergency. Get crew to medical facility.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd treat burns of different severities in an aircraft.
key_facts: Minor: cool water, cover, pain relief
Moderate: cool water, clean dressing, elevate, medical attention
Severe: cool water, cover, don't apply creams, urgent medical help
Don't apply ice directly
Don't pop blisters
Remove tight items if possible
Declare emergency for cabin fire
Prevention: caution with hot items, fire suppression available
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, First Aid Training, Medical Emergency Procedures

syllabus_desc: Describe first aid treatment for cuts and bleeding.
concept: Cuts and bleeding range from minor to life-threatening. First aid depends on severity and location. Minor cuts and bleeding (small wound, minor bleeding): wash with soap and water if available. Apply direct pressure with clean cloth for a few minutes. Pressure stops most minor bleeding. Once bleeding stops, apply clean dressing. If bleeding restarts, reapply pressure. Monitor for infection. Moderate bleeding (significant blood loss, won't stop with pressure): direct firm pressure with multiple cloth layers. Don't look under the cloth; keep pressure on. Elevate wounded area if possible. If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top, don't remove first cloth. Apply pressure for several minutes. Once bleeding slows, apply bandage. Get medical attention. Severe bleeding (arterial bleeding - bright red, spurting): direct firm pressure immediately. Use whatever's available - cloth, hand, whatever. Tourniquet if limb amputation or severe limb wound. Apply above the wound. Apply direct pressure around tourniquet. Shock management - lay flat, elevate legs. Get emergency medical help immediately. Don't remove tourniquet. In aircraft, keep first aid kit accessible. Know what's in it. Bandages, dressings, trauma supplies. For major bleeding or injury in flight: apply first aid measures, declare medical emergency, land at nearest suitable airport, get crew to medical facility. Don't try to continue flight if injured pilot or serious injury in cabin. Prevention: know where sharp objects are, secure them, be careful with tools, understand how to manage injuries quickly.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd treat different levels of bleeding in an aircraft emergency.
key_facts: Minor: wash, direct pressure, dressing
Moderate: firm pressure, elevate, bandage
Severe: direct pressure, tourniquet if needed, shock management
Don't remove cloth during pressure
Add cloth on top if needed
Tourniquet for severe limb wounds
Declare emergency for major bleeding
First aid kit accessible and known
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, First Aid Training, Medical Emergency Procedures

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of carrying a first aid kit on board an aircraft.
concept: First aid kit is critical for managing medical emergencies in flight. Importance: response time. In an aircraft, you can't call emergency services immediately. You're flying to a medical facility. In the meantime, you manage the injury with what's on board. Supplies. A kit has supplies for basic first aid: bandages for cuts, pressure dressings for bleeding, pain relief, shock management supplies, burns treatment. Without a kit, you're improvising. Improvising is less effective and slower. Legal requirement. CAR Part 135 requires first aid kit on board. Part 91 doesn't specify but strongly recommended. Professional operations and good practice require it. Passenger expectations. Passengers expect a first aid kit to exist. If someone's injured and there's no kit, passengers question your preparation and fitness to operate. Know your kit. You should know what's in your kit, where it is, and how to use it. Regular check: kits expire. Medical supplies have expiration dates. Replenish as needed. Size and type depends on aircraft and crew size. Larger aircraft with more crew and passengers need larger or more complete kits. Single-pilot aircraft might have smaller kit but still should have basics. In emergency: first aid kit plus your knowledge means you can manage injuries effectively, getting wounded to medical facility in best possible condition. It's fundamental part of flight preparation.
exam_tip: Explain what should be in a first aid kit and why each item is important.
key_facts: Manages injuries while flying to medical facility
Provides necessary supplies for treatment
Required for Part 135; recommended for all operations
Must know contents, location, and how to use
Check expiration dates regularly
Size appropriate to aircraft and crew
Fundamental part of flight preparation
Passenger expectation and professionalism
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 135, Medical Emergency Procedures

syllabus_desc: List the priorities of survival in a remote area.
concept: If you crash or land in a remote area, survival priorities are: shelter (protection from elements - hypothermia is leading killer), water (dehydration and injury), signaling for rescue (someone needs to know where you are), fire (warmth, signaling, morale), food (lower priority unless survival is extended). Immediate priorities: get everyone accounted for and evaluated for injuries. Move to safety (away from crash site if dangerous). Assess water availability. Build shelter before dark if possible. Signaling: stay with aircraft if possible (it's easier to spot than person on ground). Make aircraft visible: bright colors, mirrors to reflect sunlight. Radio - declare mayday, give position. Personal locator beacon if available. Fire: gather wood before dark. Build shelter near fire location but upwind from smoke. Water: find fresh water source (streams, rain collection). Don't drink stagnant water without purifying (boiling if possible). Food: lower priority if water available. Edible plants, insects depend on location. Rest: people are less capable if exhausted. Take turns on watch and shelter building. Morale: keeping people calm and purposeful improves survival. Working together, having a plan - these help. TAIC and survival stories emphasize staying with aircraft (easier to find) and signaling for rescue (someone will be looking). Don't wander in remote areas; stay put and wait for rescue.
exam_tip: Explain survival priorities and specific actions for a remote area crash.
key_facts: Shelter: protection from elements, prevent hypothermia
Water: dehydration is major risk
Signaling: stay with aircraft, use beacons, radio
Fire: warmth and signaling
Food: lower priority initially
Stay with aircraft if possible
Gather resources before dark
Work together, maintain morale
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Survival Training, TAIC Reports

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of adequate preparation for a flight over remote areas.
concept: Remote area flying - Fiordland, Southern Alps, West Coast, Coromandel backcountry - carries survival risks. Preparation is critical. Navigation: know your position accurately. GPS is good but can fail. Backup navigation: map, compass, paper chart knowledge. Know where you are and where you're going. Communication: position reporting. Someone on ground knows your flight plan and your position. In emergency, they'll alert rescue when you're overdue. PLBs (Personal Locator Beacons) or ELTs (Emergency Locator Transmitters) help rescuers find you. Survival equipment: depends on area. Remote areas require more. Emergency supplies: first aid, water, energy food, signaling equipment. Appropriate for the terrain and likely scenarios. Clothing: appropriate for remote area weather. Mountains are cold; coastal areas are wet and windy. Plan for worst-case weather. Knowledge: understand the terrain, hazards, what to expect. Know how long search and rescue would take to reach you. Have realistic expectations. Flight planning: fuel reserves for remote areas should be generous. Know nearest suitable landing areas. Fuel for diversion. Alternative routes. Don't fly into remote areas on marginal fuel. Communication plan: file flight plan, check in regularly if possible. Someone knows where you are and when you should arrive. Time of useful consciousness in extreme conditions is limited. Preparation and knowledge mean you're not caught unprepared. Proper preparation - flight plan, communication, survival equipment, knowledge - is the difference between managed emergency and disaster.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd prepare for a flight over remote terrain.
key_facts: Know position accurately: GPS and backup navigation
Communication: position reporting, PLBs, ELTs
Survival equipment: appropriate to terrain and season
Clothing: appropriate for worst-case weather
Knowledge: understand terrain and hazards
Generous fuel reserves
Nearest landing areas identified
Flight plan and communication: someone knows where you are
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Survival Training, Remote Area Operations

syllabus_desc: Describe basic water and food procurement methods.
concept: In survival situation, water is critical. Food is secondary. Water: find natural sources - streams, rivers, springs. Collect rainwater. Dig for groundwater if available. Purify if possible: boil (kills bacteria and parasites), use purification tablets if available, filter through cloth though this is less reliable than boiling. In mountains or forested areas, follow downslope to find water sources. Don't drink stagnant water unless desperate. Dehydration's a killer but drinking contaminated water can be worse. Food: lower priority initially. People can survive weeks without food but only days without water. Edible plants: depends on area. Southern NZ has berries, wild edible plants in some areas. West Coast has shellfish in coastal areas. Don't eat unfamiliar plants without being certain they're safe. Insects: high in protein. Grasshoppers, ants, beetle larvae are edible. Bugs are safer bet than unknown plants. Fishing or trapping: requires tools and skill. Improvise fishing hook from wire or sharp objects. Traps for small animals if materials available. Cooking and safety: cook food thoroughly if possible. Boiling kills pathogens. Fire management: gathering fuel, maintaining fire takes energy. If fire for signaling or cooking, do it. If calories-negative (using more energy to gather fuel than food provides), consider if worthwhile. Priorities: shelter, water, fire, signaling, food. Get the big ones right - shelter prevents hypothermia, water prevents dehydration, signaling gets you rescued. Food can wait if necessary. Realistic expectations: survival food might not be tasty or abundant. Be grateful for what you find. TAIC reports emphasize: stay with aircraft, focus on being found rather than self-rescue, water and shelter before food.
exam_tip: Describe water and food procurement in a specific remote area.
key_facts: Water: find sources, purify by boiling
Rainwater: collect if possible
Food: secondary to water and shelter
Edible plants: only eat if certain
Insects: safer than unknown plants
Fishing and trapping: require tools and skill
Cooking: thoroughly cook food
Priorities: water, shelter, fire, signaling before food
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Survival Training, Remote Area Operations

syllabus_desc: Describe basic shelter requirements.
concept: Shelter prevents hypothermia and weather exposure. Basic shelter requirements: protection from wind and rain. In mountains or backcountry, weather can be harsh and deadly. Hypothermia kills faster than hunger or thirst. Types of shelter: use natural shelter if available - caves, rock overhangs, dense trees. Improvise if not: debris shelter (pile branches and leaves for insulation), lean-to (branches against support, covered with leaves or materials), makeshift tarp shelter if you have tarp or parachute. Position: choose location away from hazards (avalanche areas, flood zones, falling rocks). Near water but not in flood zone. Near fire if building one. Location on high ground visible to rescue if possible, but shelter from wind. Insulation: if hypothermia risk, insulation from ground is critical. Use debris, leaves, branches to create barrier between body and cold ground. Pile materials for warmth if needed. Ventilation: if using fire inside shelter (dangerous), ensure ventilation to prevent CO poisoning. Windows or gaps for air flow. Size: smaller shelter retains heat better than large shelter. Don't waste energy on larger-than-necessary shelter unless multiple people. Heat: fire nearby if possible (safely). Other people's body heat if shelter is small. Moisture: keep shelter dry. Wet clothes kill through hypothermia faster than cold air. Change clothes if possible, hang wet items to dry. Signaling: if shelter built, make it visible to rescuers if possible. Bright colors, positioning on slopes visible from air. Realism: perfect shelter isn't always possible. Something is better than nothing. Protection from worst of weather is goal. Survival stories emphasize: build shelter early (before dark, before exhausted), insulate from ground, stay dry. These things prevent fatal hypothermia.
exam_tip: Describe how you'd build shelter in a specific remote area terrain.
key_facts: Protection from wind and rain
Hypothermia is leading killer
Use natural shelter if available
Improvise: debris shelter, lean-to, tarp
Position: near water, away from hazards
Insulation from ground critical
Keep dry: wet promotes hypothermia
Signaling: make visible to rescuers if possible
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Survival Training, TAIC Survival Reports

syllabus_desc: Describe signalling methods for attracting rescue services.
concept: If you crash or land in remote area, search and rescue will be looking for you. Signaling helps them find you. Methods: radio - declare mayday, give position if you can. Radio helps rescue services know your location and send appropriate response. Maintain communication until rescue arrives. ELT (Emergency Locator Transmitter): aircraft are equipped with these. Transmitter broadcasts on emergency frequency (121.5 MHz and 406 MHz). Rescuers receive signal and can triangulate location. Activate if time permits during emergency. PLB (Personal Locator Beacon): if you have personal beacon, activate. Broadcasts on emergency frequency. Gives rescue services your location. Signaling mirror: reflects sunlight to aircraft. Visible for miles in clear weather. Effective day-time signaling. Wave bright cloth or use parachute to signal. Fire or smoke: visible from air, especially smoke (green branches create smoke). Build fire in clearing or high point visible from air. Three of anything is universal distress signal: three fires, three smoke columns, three gun shots, three anything. Light: at night, flashlight or fire visible from air. Flash SOS if possible (three dots, three dashes, three dots). Aircraft landing lights visible from air. Markers: arrange debris or rocks in large 'X' or 'SOS' on ground visible from air. Large and obvious. Noise: if you hear aircraft, make noise - shouts, whistle, flares if available. Stay with aircraft if possible. Aircraft is easier to spot than person on ground. If you must leave aircraft, mark location so rescuers can find it. Don't wander; stay put. Search and rescue expects you to be near aircraft and will focus search there. Positioning: high ground visible from air is better than low ground hidden by trees. But shelter and safety are priority; being found but dead of exposure isn't good outcome. Balance visibility with protection.
exam_tip: Describe signaling methods you'd use in a remote area crash situation.
key_facts: Radio: declare mayday, maintain communication
ELT: broadcasts on emergency frequency
PLB: personal locator beacon
Mirror: reflects sunlight, visible for miles
Fire and smoke: visible from air
Three of anything: universal distress signal
Aircraft: stay with it, easier to spot than person
Mark position: 'X' or 'SOS' visible from air
High ground: better visibility if safe
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25, Survival Training, Search and Rescue Information
